OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:

Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 46-year-old male with HIV and hepatitis C coinfection (clinical background may be incidental).  
- Incidental bilateral osseous processes projecting from posterior iliac bones on CT.  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation on knee radiographs.  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature on elbow radiographs.  
- Hypoplastic fingernails on physical exam.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome (NPS):  
- Pros: Classic triad includes nail dysplasia (hypoplastic fingernails), absent or hypoplastic patellae, and elbow anomalies including radial head dislocation and hypoplasia. Also, iliac horns (posterior iliac projections) are pathognomonic.  
- Cons: None significant; findings fit perfectly.  

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:  
- Pros: Can have nail anomalies and patellar abnormalities.  
- Cons: Usually includes hypoplasia or aplasia of the fifth fingernail, intellectual disability, and characteristic facial features; no iliac horns or elbow radial head dislocation typical.  

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:  
- Pros: Characterized by microtia, patellar aplasia/hypoplasia, and dwarfism.  
- Cons: Nail hypoplasia not a hallmark; no iliac horns or radial head dislocation.  

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:  
- Pros: Features include patellar aplasia/hypoplasia, limb anomalies, and facial features.  
- Cons: Typically includes severe skeletal anomalies, but iliac horns and nail hypoplasia less characteristic; radial head dislocation uncommon.  

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:  
- Pros: Patellar anomalies, genital anomalies, developmental delay.  
- Cons: No nail hypoplasia; iliac horn absent; radial head dislocation not typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome is best supported by the classic combination of hypoplastic nails, patellar hypoplasia with luxation, radial head hypoplasia and dislocation, and bilateral iliac horns (posterior iliac projections). These findings are pathognomonic for NPS.  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome and Genitopatellar syndrome have some overlapping features but lack iliac horns and nail anomalies.  
3) Coffin–Siris and Meier–Gorlin syndromes do not explain all findings, especially iliac horns, which is key.  

<final> Nail–patella syndrome </final><|return|>
options: Nail–patella syndrome | Coffin–Siris syndrome | Meier–Gorlin syndrome | Coxopodopatellar syndrome | Genitopatellar syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 81-year-old male with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and recent onset orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea.
- Elevated troponins and third-degree AV block on ECG.
- Echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
- PET-CT reveals large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue mass involving right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove, sparing the left ventricle; also hypermetabolic nodular lesion at left lung base.
- Contrast-enhanced CT confirms infiltrative hypodense myocardial mass as described, with right coronary artery encircled, moderate pericardial effusion, and solid nodule in left lower lobe.
- Lung nodule biopsied for histology.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Angiosarcoma:
   - Pros: Common primary malignant cardiac tumor in elderly adults; typically involves right atrium or right ventricle; aggressive infiltrative mass with pericardial effusion; hypermetabolic on PET.
   - Cons: Usually involves right atrium predominantly; here mass involves right atrium and right ventricle and interatrial septum, which can be consistent; no specific mention of hemorrhagic or cavitating features typical for angiosarcoma.

2) Ig-G4 related disease:
   - Pros: Can involve cardiovascular structures; causes infiltrative soft tissue and pericardial involvement.
   - Cons: Typically not hypermetabolic on PET as aggressively as malignancy; usually not forming a large discrete myocardial mass; rarity of myocardial nodules; does not explain lung nodule hypermetabolism or third-degree AV block.

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma:
   - Pros: Rare but known to present with right-sided cardiac infiltration; often in older patients or those immunocompromised; can cause conduction abnormalities and pericardial effusion; PET often intensely hypermetabolic; may involve both atria and ventricles.
   - Cons: Less common than sarcoma; lung nodule could represent metastasis or separate lesion; biopsy needed for confirmation.

4) ErdheimâChester disease:
   - Pros: Rare systemic non-Langerhans histiocytosis; can involve heart (pericardium, right heart structures), pericardial effusion; systemic disease.
   - Cons: Typically shows symmetric osteosclerotic lesions; myocardial involvement usually peri-infarct or cortical; not usually presenting as a large hypermetabolic myocardial mass; lung nodules possible but rarely as a primary cardiac mass.

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma:
   - Pros: Can arise in myocardium as primary malignant tumor; aggressive infiltrative mass; PET hypermetabolism.
   - Cons: Rare; typically involves left heart more often; no specific tendency for right-side predominance; biopsy usually needed.

C) Ranking:
1) Primary cardiac lymphoma: Fits age, involvement of right heart structures, conduction abnormalities, PET hypermetabolism, and lung nodule which may represent metastasis or separate lesion.
2) Angiosarcoma: Also fits location and PET findings but less commonly involves interatrial septum and myocardium diffusely; pericardial effusion and AV block are possible.
3) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma: Possible but less common presentation.
4) Erdheim-Chester disease: Less likely due to pattern of myocardial infiltration and PET findings.
5) Ig-G4 related disease: Least likely given imaging and clinical presentation.

Thus, the most consistent diagnosis is Primary cardiac lymphoma, considering the aggressive PET hypermetabolic infiltrative mass of the right chambers with conduction block and pericardial effusion, plus lung nodule.

</analysis><final>Primary cardiac lymphoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, and epigastric pain for 7 days.  
- Laboratory: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia, mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase.  
- Imaging:  
  - Ultrasound: Hepatomegaly with a large left-lobe liver abscess (350 cc) with signs of impending rupture.  
  - Doppler US shows absent flow and echogenic material suggestive of thrombosis at the porta hepatis (portosplenic confluence), with collateral vessel formation indicating cavernous transformation.  
  - Intrahepatic biliary dilatation and intrahepatic venovenous collaterals noted between hepatic veins.  
  - CECT confirms abscess and thrombosis, plus narrowing and thrombus in a short segment of IVC.  
  - Pancreatic parenchymal varices seen (head and body), draining into splenic vein.  
  - MRCP shows biliary obstruction distal to CHD, and prominent left hepatic bile ducts.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: Presence of portal vein thrombosis, collateral vessels, and varices; portal hypertension signs.  
   - Cons: Age and acute presentation with abscess and infection suggests acute process, not chronic cirrhosis. No mention of cirrhotic morphology or fibrosis on imaging; hepatic abscess is unusual primary presentation in cirrhosis without prior known disease.  

2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: Intrahepatic venovenous collaterals seen, which can be seen in vascular malformations.  
   - Cons: No explicit imaging findings of AVM like early venous filling or nidus; clinical context is infection, not vascular malformation. Abscess and thrombosis suggest septic process, not AVM.  

3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Key features include thrombosis at porta hepatis (portosplenic confluence) - classic for pylephlebitis (septic thrombosis); large liver abscess likely source of infection; portal vein thrombosis, cavernous transformation, and collateral formation; pancreatic varices due to venous congestion secondary to portal hypertension; IVC thrombosis also fits with septic thrombophlebitis.  
   - Cons: None significant; findings support this diagnosis excellently.  

4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: Pancreatic involvement on imaging.  
   - Cons: No cystic lesions described; pancreatic varices and venous collaterals, not cystic neoplasm; clinical and imaging findings do not support cystic pancreatic lesion.  

5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
   - Pros: Pancreatic involvement mentioned.  
   - Cons: No mention of pancreatic duct dilation or mucin production; clinical picture dominated by liver abscess and septic portal thrombosis, not neoplastic process.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
2) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
3) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
5) Cystic lesions of pancreas  

Top candidate aligns with pathognomonic finding of septic thrombosis (pylephlebitis) associated with hepatic abscess, plus secondary pancreatic varices due to venous outflow obstruction.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 31-year-old male with right hemiscrotal mass, scrotal swelling for 4 weeks.  
- Clinical: Voluminous solid swelling of right hemiscrotum, retraction of penis.  
- Lab: Extremely high AFP >53,000 µg/L (very high).  
- Ultrasound: Large heterogeneous mass in right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components, vascularization; no normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis visualized on right; left testis and epididymis normal.  
- CT: Large heterogeneous mass right hemiscrotum; no metastases or lymph nodes seen.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Mixed germinal cell tumour:  
   Pros: Common testicular tumor; includes various histologic types which can elevate AFP depending on components; can present as large heterogeneous mass.  
   Cons: Typically have some metastasis at presentation; very high AFP less common than pure yolk sac tumors; no metastasis here but possible early stage.

2) Pure yolk sac tumour:  
   Pros: Known to produce very high AFP levels (often >10,000, in some cases >50,000); occurs in young males; presents as testicular mass; no metastasis can be possible early.  
   Cons: Rare in older adults (most common in children and young adolescents, though can occur in adults); but age 31 is possible.

3) Seminoma:  
   Pros: Common testicular tumor in this age group; often homogeneous hypoechoic mass; usually normal AFP (AFP is not elevated).  
   Cons: Extremely high AFP is essentially excluded with pure seminoma; AFP is normal or slightly elevated only with syncytiotrophoblast cells, but not to this level.

4) Choriocarcinoma:  
   Pros: Can elevate AFP if mixed; aggressive; vascular tumor.  
   Cons: Typically elevates beta-HCG massively rather than AFP; AFP not hallmark; rare as pure choriocarcinoma; no mention of beta-HCG levels.

5) Teratoma:  
   Pros: Can present as heterogeneous mass with cystic and solid components; can be large; can be mixed with other germ cell components.  
   Cons: AFP usually not extremely high unless mixed with yolk sac or embryonal carcinoma; by itself AFP normal; no metastasis typical but high AFP unusual.

6) Lymphoma:  
   Pros: Can present as testicular mass in older patients; no metastasis sometimes.  
   Cons: No AFP elevation; AFP is a tumor marker for germ cell tumors only; this patient has extremely high AFP, ruling out lymphoma.

C) Ranking:  
1) Pure yolk sac tumour (extremely high AFP, typical for yolk sac tumor; though age slightly older than classic, but still possible).  
2) Mixed germinal cell tumour (could explain high AFP if yolk sac component present; but pure yolk sac has higher specificity for extremely high AFP).  
3) Teratoma (less likely due to AFP).  
4) Choriocarcinoma (AFP not typical; HCG is hallmark).  
5) Seminoma (AFP not elevated).  
6) Lymphoma (AFP not elevated).  

Based on the extremely high AFP and ultrasound/CT findings, pure yolk sac tumour is most likely.  
</analysis>  
<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (

 , increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, C-reactive protein 6 mg/dL
 
 (normal value < 0.5 mg/dL)
 
 and hypoalbuminemia 2.5 g/dL.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with
 
 suspicion of lymph node metastases
 
 and the patient was referred to our oncology institute.
 
 The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma
 
 (Figure 1).
 
 In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery
 
 (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 3a, and 3b). Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

   Pros: Well-defined cystic lesion; may show rim enhancement if inflamed.

6) Anterior sacral meningocele - enhancement and fat attenuation argue against this.  
Thus, Tailgut cyst is most consistent with the findings of a multiloculated cystic mass with mixed fluid and soft tissue septations and irregular thick rim enhancement in the retrorectal/presacral area possibly extending near the ischiorectal fossa.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy. The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe. Due to the patient’s clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule. An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further. The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb), Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis, Hepatic abscess, Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma

   - Cons: Lesion morphology more complex with septa and small cysts inside, no solid enhancing components or restricted diffusion, biopsy negative for tumor cells, and morphology atypical for lymphoma metastases (usually solid or less organized cystic).

   - Cons: No restricted diffusion (common in abscess), no rim enhancement, biopsy did not show infection, no elevated inflammatory markers notably. Also, lesion walls thin, septa are internal, not typical abscess wall.

   - Pros: Cystic liver lesions with septa and complex cystic components; female patients often affected.

Thus, the best fit is Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

  Pros: Fusion and segmentation defects of vertebrae and ribs are classic; rib fusion and segmentation anomalies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An otherwise healthy 22-year-old man presents with acute respiratory symptoms and has an emergency CT scan performed, which reveals an incidental finding.
 Imaging Findings: No abnormalities are evident on a PA radiograph of the chest (Figure 1). The CT reveals no thoracic abnormalities except for two bilateral cortical defects of the undersurface of the sternal extremity of each clavicle, being slightly larger on the left side (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). An MRI is additionally performed, revealing no evidence of a pathological fracture, soft tissue mass or bone marrow oedema (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

   - Pros: Lytic lesions can appear as cortical defects.
   - Cons: Unlikely in a young healthy adult with no known primary malignancy; lesion is bilateral and symmetrical, which is unusual for metastasis; MRI shows no soft tissue mass or bone marrow edema.

2) Osteomyelitis:

3) Plasmacytoma:

4) Giant cell tumour:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

A) Key findings:
- Newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies.
- Omphalocele with liver and bowel herniation through a membrane-covered defect.
- Bladder exstrophy.
- Imperforate anus.
- Lower thoracic and lumbar vertebral fusion defects with a cystic mass having dystrophic neural elements (suggesting spinal dysraphism).
- Cranial abnormalities: low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis.
- Ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic, one pelvic).
- Midgut malrotation.
- Sacral agenesis.
- Rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities.
- Presence of multiple congenital anomalies affecting various systems (gastrointestinal, genitourinary, spinal, cranial, musculoskeletal).

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) VACTERL association:
   - Pros: VACTERL includes Vertebral anomalies (present), Anal atresia (present), Cardiovascular anomalies, TracheoEsophageal fistula, Renal anomalies (ectopic kidneys), and Limb anomalies.
   - Cons: Does not usually include omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, or ambiguous genitalia. Also, no cardiovascular anomalies or tracheoesophageal fistula explicitly mentioned.

2) Caudal regression syndrome:
   - Pros: Sacral agenesis, vertebral anomalies, and lower limb abnormalities are typical. Associated with genitourinary anomalies and spinal dysraphism.
   - Cons: Omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus are not typical. Also, cranial abnormalities and midgut malrotation are less commonly described.

3) Pentalogy of Cantrell:
   - Pros: Includes omphalocele and ventral wall defects.
   - Cons: Classic pentad involves midline defects: omphalocele, ectopia cordis, anterior diaphragmatic defect, sternal defect, and pericardial defect. No mention of ectopia cordis or pericardial defects here. Also, bladder exstrophy and spinal dysraphism are not typical.

4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome:
   - Pros: Omphalocele, organomegaly, macrosomia.
   - Cons: No mention of macrosomia or typical features like hemihyperplasia, macroglossia; bladder exstrophy and spinal anomalies are not usual.

5) OEIS complex:
   - Pros: By definition, includes Omphalocele, Exstrophy of bladder (cloacal exstrophy), Imperforate anus, and Spinal dysraphism. Also explains ambiguous genitalia and multiple musculoskeletal anomalies. The combination of omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, imperforate anus, spinal defects with a cystic mass containing neural elements, rib and vertebral anomalies, sacral agenesis, and ambiguous genitalia fits OEIS complex exactly.

C) Ranking:
1) OEIS complex - perfectly matches the combination of all described anomalies (omphalocele, bladder exstrophy, imperforate anus, spinal dysraphism).
2) Caudal regression syndrome - fits many spinal and genitourinary features but lacks omphalocele and bladder exstrophy.
3) VACTERL association - fits many but misses key omphalocele and bladder exstrophy.
4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome - insufficient classic features.
5) Pentalogy of Cantrell - partial overlap (omphalocele) but missing other cardinal features.

Thus, OEIS complex is the best fit, given presence of all four defining components plus additional associated anomalies.
</analysis>

2) Caudal regression syndrome - fits many spinal and genitourinary features but lacks omphalocele and bladder exstrophy.
3) VACTERL association - fits many but misses key omphalocele and bladder exstrophy.
4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome - insufficient classic features.
5) Pentalogy of Cantrell - partial overlap (omphalocele) but missing other cardinal features.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.

 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).

   Pros: Can cause subcutaneous cystic lesions.

4) Foreign body granuloma:
   Pros: Can cause subcutaneous cystic lesions.
   Cons: No ultrasound findings of motile tubular structures; no "filarial dance" sign.

 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.

 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (

 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).

Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.
PostDescription:

Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion. However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast. This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres to around 22x15 mm, and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures, with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 67-year-old woman with known malignant renal mass, undergoing staging CT.
- Multiple bilateral peripheral lung nodules with tissular densities.
- Nodules range from a few millimeters to 22x15 mm.
- Nodules enhance similarly to pulmonary vascular structures during arterial phase.
- Feeding vessels arise from lobar arteries; drainage into lobar veins.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1â2 pros/cons each:

1) Hypervascular lung metastases
   Pros: Patient with malignant renal mass (common to metastasize to lungs), nodules enhance, heterogeneous size.
   Cons: Metastases usually enhance but may not show discrete feeding arteries and venous drainage on arterial phase; vascular feeding vessels and drainage as described is less typical for metastases.

2) Pulmonary sequestration
   Pros: Hypervascular lesion, feeding arteries.
   Cons: Usually a single lesion with systemic arterial supply (often from aorta), not multiple small nodules; no venous drainage directly into pulmonary veins; typically congenital and isolated.

3) Bronchocoele
   Pros: Can appear as peripheral nodular opacities.
   Cons: Usually non-enhancing, air-filled dilated bronchi, not vascular enhancement with feeding vessels and venous drainage as described.

4) Pulmonary varix
   Pros: Vascular structure with venous drainage.
   Cons: Usually a single venous dilation; not multiple nodules with arterial feeding vessels; varices generally don't enhance like arteries during arterial phase.

5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm
   Pros: Vascular dilation.
   Cons: Usually larger, solitary lesion; may enhance but multiple small lesions unlikely; feeding artery and venous drainage typical, but multiple lesions uncommon.

6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (PAVMs)
   Pros: Multiple small peripheral lesions with feeding arteries from lobar arteries and venous drainage; hyperenhancing on arterial phase similar to vascular structures; common in malignancy patients but can be congenital or acquired.
   Cons: No major contradictions.

7) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications
   Pros: Vascular anomalies with abnormal connections.
   Cons: Usually systemic artery to pulmonary artery fistula; fewer and larger than described; not multiple small nodules.

C) Ranking:
1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations - best fits multiple small peripheral nodules with feeding arteries from lobar arteries and venous drainage, hyperenhancement arterial phase.
2) Hypervascular lung metastases - plausible due to malignancy, but vascular feeding vessels and organized venous drainage in nodules less typical.
3) Others less consistent.

<final>Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.

- 39-year-old male with tibial, fibular fractures and traumatic brain injury (TBI) from motor vehicle accident.

- No new trauma; patient bedridden.
- Multifocal subarachnoid hemorrhage noted.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Calcific tendinitis
   - Pros: Can cause periarticular calcification; shoulder involvement possible.
   - Cons: Typically unilateral; involves tendons not leading to extensive mature heterotopic bone; no trauma or neurologic injury related.
2) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification
   - Pros: Strong association with TBI; bilateral mature heterotopic ossification in the shoulder (common site); patient bedridden after TBI; classic presentation 6 months post-injury.
   - Cons: None significant.
3) Myositis ossificans
   - Pros: Can cause heterotopic ossification in muscle; often after trauma.
   - Cons: Typically unilateral, localized, often immature or with zoning phenomenon; does not usually involve joints extensively; no mention of muscle mass; bilateral joint involvement less common.
4) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease
   - Pros: Could cause soft tissue calcifications.
   - Cons: Usually more diffuse or systemic; not limited to mature heterotopic bone formation at joints; no systemic or metabolic symptoms described.
5) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva
   - Pros: Heterotopic ossification in muscles.
   - Cons: Rare genetic disease; usually presents in childhood; progressive; not related to trauma or TBI; unilateral bilateral simultaneous ossification after trauma unlikely.
6) Tumoral calcinosis
   - Pros: Soft tissue calcification.
   - Cons: Typically periarticular, but not mature heterotopic bone formation; also not related to trauma/TBI; often involves hip or elbow, rare in shoulder joint; no trauma history.
7) Sarcoma
   - Pros: Bone formation in soft tissue can be seen.
   - Cons: No mass lesion described; bilateral symmetric ossification; no malignant features; sarcoma unlikely.

C) Ranking:
1) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification (matches classic presentation after TBI with extensive bilateral mature bone in shoulder region)
2) Myositis ossificans (post-trauma, but less likely due to bilateral joint involvement and mature ossification)
3) Other options less fitting given clinical and imaging context.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

1) Intra-tendinous calcifications following trauma

   - Pro: Gout can cause intra-tendinous deposits.

   - Pro: Possible but rare and non-specific.

   - Pro: Chondrocalcinosis is a hallmark of CPPD; common in elderly; US shows rhomboid-shaped calcifications/linear deposits with acoustic shadowing in tendons; bilateral involvement common.
   - Con: None significant.
5) Hydroxyapatite deposits
   - Pro: Hydroxyapatite calcific tendinitis can affect tendons.
   - Con: Typically seen in rotator cuff tendons; Achilles involvement rare; deposition pattern and clinical context less typical.

4) Hydroxyapatite depositsâUncommon in Achilles tendons, and pattern less consistent.
5) Intra-tendinous calcifications.following traumaâNo trauma history, bilateral symmetric lesions rare in trauma.

Overall, Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease best explains the clinical and imaging findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1]. It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci. No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Fibrous hamartoma of infancy, Rhabdomyosarcoma, Lipoblastoma, Liposarcoma, Lipoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 4-month-old male infant with a painless palpable mass on the arm, slowly growing since 1.5 months old.  
- Ultrasound: poorly defined, hyperechoic relative to muscle, subcutaneous tissue, serpentine hypoechoic bands with hyperechoic border, low vascularity on Doppler.  
- MRI: non-encapsulated soft tissue mass, subcutaneous tissue resting on fascia, no muscle involvement. Isointense to muscle on T1 and T1 fat-saturated sequences with interspersed fat foci, no gadolinium enhancement.  
- Clinical: painless, no inflammation, firm palpable mass.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Fibrous hamartoma of infancy  
   Pros: Typical age (infancy), location (subcutaneous), slow-growing painless mass, infiltrative but non-encapsulated lesion, mixed echotexture on US with fibrous and fatty components, low vascularity, isointense T1 with fatty foci, no enhancement.  
   Cons: None significant; fits well.  

2) Rhabdomyosarcoma  
   Pros: Can occur in infants, soft tissue mass.  
   Cons: Usually more aggressive, with enhancement on MRI, well-defined or infiltrative but more vascular, often involves muscle or deeper tissues, rapid growth, younger age but less common as a solitary subcutaneous mass without muscle involvement and no enhancement.  

3) Lipoblastoma  
   Pros: Occurs in infants, fatty tumor.  
   Cons: Typically multinodular or lobulated fatty mass, often encapsulated or delineated, typically isointense to fat rather than muscle, usually hypervascular, may have septations, often shows enhancement of septa. This lesion has mixed soft tissue and fat, but not classic lipoblastoma.  

4) Liposarcoma  
   Pros: Soft tissue mass, fatty components.  
   Cons: Extremely rare in infants; usually seen in older children/adults; malignant features such as enhancement, infiltration, and vascularity usually present. Also, not typical location.  

5) Lipoma  
   Pros: Benign soft tissue fatty mass.  
   Cons: Usually encapsulated, homogeneous, fat signal on MRI, no mixed echogenicity or serpentine hypoechoic bands, usually well-defined, slower or stable growth, not typically seen in infants.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Fibrous hamartoma of infancy – matches age, site, imaging characteristics, and clinical presentation. Pathognomonic features are present.  
2) Lipoblastoma – possible but less likely due to imaging and clinical features.  
3) Lipoma – less likely given age and imaging.  
4) Rhabdomyosarcoma – imaging and clinical do not support aggressive or vascular lesion.  
5) Liposarcoma – extremely unlikely given age and lack of malignant imaging features.  
<final>Fibrous hamartoma of infancy</final><|return|>
options: Fibrous hamartoma of infancy | Rhabdomyosarcoma | Lipoblastoma | Liposarcoma | Lipoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydronephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Post-transplant renal artery stent, Post-transplant renal vein stenting, Post-transplant ureteral stent removal, Post-transplant ureteral stent placement
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 63-year-old male with renal transplant from cadaveric donor 3 months ago due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis  
- Clinical presentation: worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia  
- Ultrasound: grade III hydronephrosis  
- Procedure: unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy under fluoroscopy and IV sedation  
- Antegrade pyelography: stricture at ureteral-bladder anastomosis, tortuosity of the ureter causing occlusive narrowing, failure to pass contrast into bladder, ureteric bud not visualized despite multiple fluoroscopic angles  
- 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage placed successfully in renal pelvis  
- Second attempt planned in 2 weeks  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Post-transplant renal artery stent  
  - Pros: Transplant patients may need arterial stents; renal artery is relevant in transplant.  
  - Cons: No mention of arterial intervention or vascular imaging findings; findings describe ureteric problems and hydronephrosis, not arterial stenosis or stent.  
- Post-transplant renal vein stenting  
  - Pros: Renal vein stenosis can cause allograft dysfunction; venous involvement can occur.  
  - Cons: No imaging findings or clinical signs consistent with venous outflow obstruction; focus is on ureteral obstruction with hydronephrosis and stricture.  
- Post-transplant ureteral stent removal  
  - Pros: Ureteral stents are commonly placed post-transplant; removal is a standard postoperative step.  
  - Cons: The description indicates attempts to navigate a stricture and place a nephrostomy, suggesting the ureter is obstructed and not related to stent removal. No mention of stent removal procedure.  
- Post-transplant ureteral stent placement  
  - Pros: Clinical scenario of early post-transplant complications includes ureteral strictures; initial management often involves antegrade pyelography and placement of nephrostomy, followed by ureteral stenting; the case describes unsuccessful attempts to pass contrast due to stricture, and placement of nephrostomy, which is often a bridge to stent placement.  
  - Cons: The text mentions a "failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing," implying the stent placement was not successful yet, but a nephrostomy was placed pending a second attempt.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement (most consistent with management of ureteral strictures and hydronephrosis with nephrostomy and future stenting)  
2) Post-transplant renal artery stent (unlikely given no vascular findings)  
3) Post-transplant renal vein stent (less likely than artery stent)  
4) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal (no evidence or mention of stent removal)  

Hence, the best diagnosis is "Post-transplant ureteral stent placement."  
</analysis><final> Post-transplant ureteral stent placement </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

OriginalDescription:

 Imaging Findings: MRI scan of the left knee (Figures 1, 2, and 3) demonstrated diffuse T2 bright and iso-hypotense T1 tissue infiltrating the subcutaneous and intermuscular planes at the posterior aspect of the knee and reaching the periosteal surface of the distal femur, causing bone dysplasia with widening of the posterior intercondylar notch. CT imaging also demonstrated the marked widening of the intercondylar notch (Figures 4 and 5).

Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 58-year-old male with worsening nausea for the past 72 hours presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain radiating to the back in the context of 4 recent episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). An anechoic collection measuring 3.3 x 1.2 x 8.6 cm was observed in the intermuscular fascial plane, between the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with no increased vascularity noted on the Doppler study (Figures 7a and 7b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months,
 
 previously without comorbidities,
 
 presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and
 
 an onset of diabetes
 
 . Nivolumab was suspended till lipase decreased. After the reintroduction of nivolumab, another elevation of lipase
 
 and abdominal pain
 
 were
 
 noted.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity (Figure 1), and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported (Figure 2), as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney (Figure 3).
 
 At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion (Figures 4a, 4b, 5, 6a, and 6b). Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. There were no fluid collections, free abdominal fluid or adenopathy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

- Presence of "extra-pleural fat sign" on CT indicates an extra-pleural process.  
- No contrast given due to renal insufficiency, so bleeding could not be excluded.  
- MRI T1-weighted: hyperintense lesion consistent with blood products.  
- MRI T2-weighted: mildly hyperintense, consistent with subacute hemorrhage or organized hematoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old female was admitted to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of constipation, drooling, and left eye strabismus due to oculomotor nerve paralysis. Further physical examination without clinically significant findings. Initial laboratory tests upon admission showed white blood count (WBC), neutrophils, and lymphocytes within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: An initial contrast-enhanced CT scan of the brain (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed a lesion that extended to cranial structures and temporal lobes, involving deep neck spaces.
 
 An initial contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) showed pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct calibre, bilateral renal cortical lesions, and a right paraspinal lesion invading the spinal canal at L2–L3 with nerve roots involvement, suggestive of metastasis. Pelvic lymphadenopathies were identified.
 
 A 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) revealed a smaller lesion compared to the previous CT, with decreased intracranial and cerebral extension and a reduced cerebral and cerebellar volume.
 
 The 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) showed no pleural thickening areas or subpleural nodules. The pancreas with a size reduction of the focal lesions. The pancreatic duct calibre was preserved, and the kidneys had no infiltrative lesions. Generalised reduction of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The paraspinal lesion was not visible. A not previously identified generalised fatty infiltration was shown.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: In an ambulatory appointment, a 29-year-old female patient presented with chronic and persistent cough. No smoking history was referred, and the physical examination was unremarkable. An ambulatory chest x-ray was performed in our institution and further evaluation with computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), was later requested.
 Imaging Findings: The chest x-ray (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed an opacity, with rounded and well-defined borders, localised in the posterior and medial regions of the right middle lung field.
 
 Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT /Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) revealed in the right paravertebral region, next to the posteromedial aspect of the 7
 
 th
 
 and 8
 
 costal arches, a large nodular lesion, with 47 x 49 x 41 mm (L x AP x T), ovoid with regular and well-defined borders, heterogeneous and with marked peripheral contrast uptake. It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography
 
 , as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to
 
 nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures.
 
 The contrast-enhanced thoracic MRI images (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) confirmed the localisation and nature of the mass, further characterising it as a soft tissue lesion showing heterogeneity of signal in the T2-weighted sequences, with areas of interstitial hyperintensity and markedly heterogeneous uptake after contrast administration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient presented to our department for routine follow-up imaging after left radical orchiectomy 19 years earlier due to a testicular germ cell tumour, and with retroperitoneal recurrence 11 years after surgery. He had a previous history of left orchidopexy during childhood to repair cryptorchidism.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, who underwent a left radical orchiectomy for a testicular germ cell tumour, the abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) revealed tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter, representing a retroperitoneal splenic cord connecting the spleen to the remaining mesonephric structure in the left lower quadrant (Figures 1a to 1i). Additionally, this condition was associated with congenital absence of lower limbs (Figure 2).
 
 Retrospectively, the described abdominal features were considered stable compared to the oldest examination available in our database, which was performed at the time of the retroperitoneal recurrence (Figures 3a to 3f).

- History of left orchidopexy (cryptorchidism repair) in childhood.
- Associated congenital absence of lower limbs.
- Imaging findings are stable compared to prior images.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Splenogonadal fusion
   - Pros: Tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter connecting to mesonephric structures aligns with splenogonadal fusion, which is a congenital anomaly where splenic tissue connects or fuses with gonadal or mesonephric structures. Usually left-sided. May be associated with cryptorchidism and other abnormalities. The history of orchidopexy and testicular tumour supports this diagnosis. This is a typical and classic presentation.
   - Cons: None significant; findings are pathognomonic.
   
2) Splenomegaly
   - Pros: Presence of splenic tissue, but no mention of enlarged spleen or diffuse splenomegaly.
   - Cons: Splenomegaly typically presents as homogeneous splenomegaly, not tubular ectopic splenic tissue. The description of tubular-shaped splenic tissue connecting spleen to mesonephric structure is not consistent with splenomegaly. Also, no mention of systemic causes.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old female with poor feeding toleration, aspirating all consistencies of food, with failure to thrive, was planned for gastrostomy tube placement, and an upper gastrointestinal study was requested for the anatomic assessment.
 Imaging Findings: A fluoroscopic upper gastrointestinal study was done by injection of barium via the indwelling enteric tube. The stomach exhibited a normal appearance and was normally emptied through a normal pylorus, duodenal bulb, and descending duodenum (Figure 1). After an initial descent of the duodenum, the third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb and then completes its transverse course and reaches the left upper abdomen (Figures 2 and 3).

1) Duodenum inversum:
   - Pros: Described by an abnormal course of the third part of the duodenum ascending rather than crossing midline, with a course parallel and posterior to descending duodenum, then crossing in left upper abdomen.
   - Cons: No mention of typical associated symptoms or complications, but imaging fits classic description of duodenum inversum as per Radiology literature.

2) Redundant duodenum:
   - Pros: May cause abnormal course of duodenum due to excessive length.
   - Cons: Typically presents with elongation and excessive loops, not specific abnormal directionality as described. No mention of redundancy in the study.

3) Intestinal malrotation:
   - Pros: Could cause abnormal positioning of duodenum and intestines.

No mention of corkscrew or twisted bowel.
