OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old female patient presented with exertional dyspnoea, weakness, abdominal pain, and lower limb swelling persisting for a month. There was no history of fever. Initial ultrasound investigation revealed mild ascites, multiple small abdominal lymphadenopathies and minimal pericardial effusion. Laboratory results showed haemoglobin of 12.2g/dL, platelet count of 105×109/L and white blood cell count of 3.32×10
 
 9
 
 /L. A 12-lead ECG revealed prolonged P waves, abnormal Q waves, poor R wave progression, and T wave abnormalities (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography revealed mild pericardial effusion, grade II diastolic dysfunction, right ventricle with segmental aneurysmal dilation and apical retraction, mild mitral regurgitation, and hyperechoic mass at the left ventricular apex, indicative of fibrofatty tissue deposition. While the right ventricle (RV) showed localised enlargement due to aneurysm formation, the overall restrictive physiology remained evident (Figures 2a and 2b). No thrombus was detected on echocardiography and cardiac MRI. Chest X-ray showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette and elevated diaphragms.
 
 The patient was referred for a cardiac MRI in our department. Cardiac MRI, done with cine imaging, perfusion, and early and late gadolinium-enhanced (LGE) PSIR sequences on MAGNETOM Vida 3T scanner revealed ascites, pericardial effusion, enlarged right atrium, retraction of the RV apex (RV apical notch), RV aneurysm, obliteration of the left ventricular (LV) apex on True FISP (true fast imaging with steady-state free precession) cine imaging (Figure 3). The LV end-diastolic volume was 69 mL, and the end-systolic volume was 28 mL. Similarly, the RV end-diastolic volume measured 70 mL, with an end-systolic volume of 39 mL, indicating relatively small chamber volumes. LGE imaging demonstrated subendocardial fibrosis with fat infiltration at both the LV and RV apices, resulting in a V-shaped enhancement pattern in each ventricle (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old Malagasy boy consulted for a fever that had been evolving for two months, with a notion of trauma and pain in the right upper quadrant as well as chronic diarrhoea. A parasitological examination of the stool found eggs of
 
 Schistosoma mansoni
 
 . An ultrasound and a CT scan were then ordered.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), there were multiple confluent hypoechoic nodular formations, some of which have peripheral hyperechoic walls in segment VII of the liver. On CT scan (Figures 2 and 3), the formation corresponds to multiple hypodense fluid collections with thick peripheral enhancing walls with peri-lesional oedema in segment VII of the liver. There was an extracapsular extension into the hepato-renal space. The liver was enlarged, measuring 187 mm in the midclavicular line. The spleen was also increased in size, measuring 160 mm in height. The rest of the examination was considered normal.

A) Key Findings:  
- 12-year-old boy with two months of fever, pain in right upper quadrant, chronic diarrhea.  
- Stool exam positive for Schistosoma mansoni eggs.  
- Ultrasound: multiple confluent hypoechoic nodular formations with peripheral hyperechoic walls in liver segment VII.  
- CT scan: multiple hypodense fluid collections with thick peripheral enhancing walls, peri-lesional edema, extracapsular extension into hepatorenal space.  
- Liver enlargement (187 mm).  
- Spleen enlargement (160 mm).  
- No other abnormal findings reported.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  

1) Hydatid cyst:  
Pros: Multiple hypodense lesions with thick enhancing walls and peripheral hyperechoic walls on US can be consistent with hydatid cysts. Peripheral rim enhancement with septations is typical. Common in endemic areas.  
Cons: No mention of typical daughter cysts, calcifications, or "water lily sign." Extracapsular extension and peri-lesional edema are less characteristic. Also, no mention of typical epidemiology or exposure. 

2) Liver pyogenic abscess:  
Pros: Multiple fluid collections with thick, enhancing walls, peripheral edema, and extracapsular extension are classic for pyogenic liver abscesses. Fever, pain, and immunocompromised states are common. The chronicity, right upper quadrant pain, and systemic symptoms support this.  
Cons: Usually occurs in context of infections like biliary disease or sepsis; no mention but can coexist. The positive stool exam for Schistosoma does not exclude abscess as co-infection or secondary complication.

3) Hepatic adenoma:  
Pros: Usually solitary, well-circumscribed, may show heterogenous enhancement; can be multiple in rare cases. No typical history of oral contraceptives or similar risk factors.  
Cons: No typical imaging features (no fat, no hemorrhage). Multiple confluent lesions with thick walls and extracapsular extension is atypical.

4) Hepatic malignant mass (e.g., metastases or primary malignancy):  
Pros: Multiple lesions possible. Hepatomegaly and splenomegaly can be seen in advanced disease.  
Cons: Usually no peripheral rim enhancement with thick walls and no peri-lesional edema typical of abscesses. No mention of primary malignancy or weight loss. Age 12 is young for primary liver malignancy, but can have, e.g., hepatoblastoma, but imaging characteristics differ.

5) Hepatic haemangioma:  
Pros: Common benign liver lesion; multiple haemangiomas possible. 
Cons: Haemangiomas have typical "lightbulb bright" on T2 MRI, peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in on contrast. On CT, they are usually hypodense but show peripheral nodular enhancement, no thick walls or peri-lesional edema or extracapsular extension. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented to the medical OPD with complaints of ataxia, multiple episodes of falls while walking, and bed wetting for a one-year duration, with gradually progressive symptoms. On examination, the patient had shuffling gait, tremors, and cogwheel rigidity. Nerve conduction studies, parathyroid hormone assay and calcium studies were within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT head revealed bilaterally symmetrical hyperdense areas in bilateral basal ganglia, thalami, subcortical white matter, red nucleus, dentate nucleus and bilateral cerebellar hemispheres (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 MRI brain revealed bilaterally symmetrical areas that were hyperintense on T1WI, hypointense on T2WI, and showing blooming on gradient echo sequences (GRE) images in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalami (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c), bilateral red nuclei (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), bilateral dentate nuclei and cerebellar hemispheres (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c), and subcortical white matter (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c). These areas did not show restriction of diffusion on DWI images. There were discrete and confluent areas of T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in periventricular and deep white matter of bilateral frontoparietal lobes, showing no restriction of diffusion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male patient with no significant past medical background is referred for left knee pain since the age of 10, associated with knee swelling. Since then, he has always maintained complaints but lived a normal life. Plain radiograph, CT scan and MR were ordered for further investigation.
 Imaging Findings: On plain film (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d), there are periosteal and endosteal cortical thickening associated with extra-cortical bone formation along the anterior aspect of the left tibia and the medial aspect of the left femur. Areas of central medullary hyperostosis can be observed in the inferior third of the femoral diaphysis and in the femoral head. The lesions have an appearance that resembles “flowing candle wax”.
 
 CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, and 2e) allows for further acknowledgement of the changes described above.
 
 MR performed on a 1.5T MR scanner (T1 and STIR sequences in axial and coronal planes; Figures 3a and 3b) depict extensive changes with very low signal on all imaging sequences, mainly involving the left femur and tibia, with diffuse thickening of the bone cortex giving the bone surface a wavy appearance, as well as multiple intramedullary foci showing marked hypointensity, indicating a strong component of bone sclerosis. The lesions show eccentric growth, extending from the diaphysis to the epiphysis, and exceeding the cortical surface.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old woman (P1L1) presented with a two‐day history of acute pelvic pain and vomiting. There was no history of fever or vaginal bleeding. Her menstrual cycles were regular, but often accompanied by mild dysmenorrhea. No history of tubal ligation. The patient’s medical history was unremarkable, and a urinary pregnancy test was negative.
 Imaging Findings: Transvaginal sonography (TVS) revealed a convoluted, heterogeneously echogenic mass in the pouch of Douglas (POD), predominantly towards the right, showing significant surrounding inflammation. At its distal end was a well-defined, hypoechoic cystic lesion measuring approximately 3×4 cm, exhibiting peripheral mucosal infoldings with a classic cog-wheel pattern, strongly indicating tubal origin. The cyst contained homogeneous low-level internal echoes, raising suspicion for a haematosalpinx.
 
 Doppler imaging demonstrated a twisted vascular pedicle with a near-classic whirlpool pattern, along with pronounced adnexal hyperaemia. The right ovary maintained its normal anatomical position, morphology and good stromal vascularity, however, with mild periovarian hyperaemia. A small volume of echogenic free fluid was present in the POD.
 
 The left ovary, adnexa, uterus and cervix appeared unremarkable, while the endometrium exhibited features of hyperplasia.

   - Pros: Adjacent inflammation; mass in POD.
   - Cons: No fever or systemic signs; no purulent fluid; cystic lesion with twisted pedicle suggests torsion rather than infection; no abscess seen.

4) Isolated right tubal torsion with haematosalpinx:
   - Pros: Twisted vascular pedicle with whirlpool pattern; mucosal infoldings with cog-wheel pattern typical for tube; haematosalpinx present; normal ovarian appearance; acute pain and adnexal mass strongly support tubo-ovarian torsion; negative pregnancy test aligns.
   - Cons: No major cons; fits well with imaging and clinical presentation.

5) Haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with rupture:
   - Pros: Acute pain; fluid in POD.
   - Cons: Ovaries are normal on imaging; no cystic lesion in ovary; mass is tubal in origin; no mention of typical cyst wall or ovarian rupture.

C) Ranking:
1) Isolated right tubal torsion with haematosalpinx - pathognomonic whirlpool sign, cog-wheel pattern (tubal origin), haematosalpinx, normal ovary.
2) Ovarian torsion - less likely anatomy.
3) Ruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy - negative pregnancy test essentially rules out.
4) Pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian abscess - no systemic signs or abscess.
5) Haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with rupture - imaging does not support ovarian lesion.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old non-obese female with a history of cholecystectomy in the last four months presents to the emergency department with clinical signs including right upper quadrant pain, generalised jaundice, and a fever of 38.5°C. Liver function tests reveal hepatic cytolysis and cholestasis (elevated AST/ALT and ALP). An abdominal ultrasound was requested.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound with a low-frequency probe reveals the presence of multiple hyperechoic formations within the intrahepatic bile ducts, causing posterior acoustic shadowing (Figure 1) and resulting in dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 2 and 3). The common bile duct was clear.
 
 The presence of jaundice and fever raised concerns about an associated cholangitis, prompting the indication for hepatobiliary MRI. The MRI reveals more pronounced dilatation of the intrahepatic bile ducts, particularly in the left lobe of the liver, containing multiple signal-void formations consistent with the previously described biliary calculi (Figures 4 and 5). Additionally, T1-weighted sequences post-gadolinium demonstrate subtle thickening and enhancement of the intrahepatic bile duct walls along with peribiliary hepatic infiltration, secondary to the inflammation of the liver adjacent to biliary ducts caused by potential cholangitis (Figure 6).

A) Key findings:  
- 36-year-old female, post-cholecystectomy (4 months prior).  
- Clinical presentation: right upper quadrant pain, generalized jaundice, fever (38.5°C), elevated AST/ALT and ALP indicating hepatocellular injury and cholestasis.  
- Ultrasound: multiple hyperechoic formations within intrahepatic bile ducts causing posterior shadowing and intrahepatic biliary dilatation; common bile duct is clear.  
- MRI: confirms intrahepatic bile duct dilatation, especially left lobe, with multiple signal-void formations (consistent with biliary calculi), subtle thickening and enhancement of intrahepatic bile duct walls, peribiliary hepatic infiltration (inflammation), raising suspicion of cholangitis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Classical gallbladder lithiasis:  
- Pros: Presence of biliary stones is typical.  
- Cons: Patient is post-cholecystectomy, so gallbladder stones unlikely. Also, stones are intrahepatic, not gallbladder. Clinical context does not support gallbladder lithiasis.  

2) Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis:  
- Pros: Presents with recurrent fevers, intrahepatic biliary dilatation with stones, bile duct wall enhancement and peribiliary infiltration indicating infection/inflammation. History of previous infection possible.  
- Cons: Not explicitly stated recurrent episodes; single episode but fits clinical presentation with cholangitis and intrahepatic stones.  

3) Low phospholipid-associated cholelithiasis (LPAC) syndrome:  
- Pros: Young female, intrahepatic stones, cholestatic pattern, intrahepatic duct dilatation with hyperechoic material, typical presentation of LPAC. LPAC is characterized by intrahepatic cholestatic stones, recurrent cholangitis, and hepatobiliary inflammation.  
- Cons: No mention of prior recurrence or lipid abnormalities; however, patient age and clinical picture fit well.  

4) Caroli disease:  
- Pros: Causes intrahepatic bile duct dilatation with cystic spaces; predisposes to stones and cholangitis.  
- Cons: Caroli disease shows segmental or focal intrahepatic bile duct ectasia with saccular or cystic dilatations, often described as "central dot sign" on imaging; here, there is multiple stones but no mention of segmental saccular dilatation. Also, peribiliary inflammation is consistent but not specific.  

5) Primary sclerosing cholangitis:  
- Pros: Intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile duct inflammation, strictures, and beading common.  
- Cons: Typically involves both intra- and extrahepatic ducts with narrowings and strictures, not primarily stones; patient is young and female (PSC more common in men), and absence of extrahepatic duct involvement or classic ductal changes.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Low phospholipid-associated cholelithiasis (LPAC) syndrome - fits young female, intrahepatic stones, cholangitis, post-cholecystectomy, no gallbladder stones.  
2) Recurrent pyogenic cholangiohepatitis - fits symptoms but lacks history of recurrence explicitly; stones could be secondary.  
3) Caroli disease - possible but lacking typical imaging features (segmental saccular dilatation).  
4) Primary sclerosing cholangitis - unlikely due to demographics and imaging.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 53-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with an episode of dysarthria followed by a decrease in the level of consciousness, without sphincter involvement. Physical examination upon admission was unremarkable, with no signs of neurological focal deficits.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced CT shows two hypodense frontal lesions involving the cortex and the subcortical white matter with minimal surrounding oedema (Figure 1). The right lesion presents with a gyriform coarse calcification, and there is no enhancement after intravenous contrast administration (Figure 2). The left lesion produces light bone remodelling.
 
 On MRI, the lesions exhibit heterogeneous signal intensity and ill-defined margins, predominantly showing hyperintensity on T2/FLAIR sequences without communication between them through the corpus callosum (Figures 3a and 3b), and areas of hypointensity on susceptibility-weighted imaging consistent with coarse calcifications on the right side and bleeding on the left side (Figures 4a and 4b). There is no significant diffusion restriction (Figures 5a and 5b). Following contrast administration, only minimal linear enhancement is observed on both lesions (Figure 6).
 
 A perfusion study was carried out showing an increase in the cerebral blood volume (Figures 7a and 7b), and spectroscopy reveals a decrease in the N-acetylaspartate (N-Ace) peak, no elevation of lactate (LL) and an increased choline (Ch) level (Figures 8a and 8b).

A) Key findings:  
- Patient: Previously healthy 53-year-old female with transient dysarthria and decreased consciousness, no focal deficits.  
- Imaging:  
  - Non-enhanced CT shows two hypodense frontal lesions (cortex and subcortical white matter), minimal surrounding edema.  
  - Right lesion: gyriform coarse calcification, no post-contrast enhancement.  
  - Left lesion: light bone remodeling, minimal linear enhancement.  
- MRI:  
  - Both lesions show heterogeneous signal and ill-defined margins, T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, no corpus callosum communication.  
  - Right lesion shows hypointense areas on susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) compatible with coarse calcifications.  
  - Left lesion shows hypointense areas on SWI consistent with hemorrhage.  
  - No significant diffusion restriction.  
  - Perfusion MRI: increased cerebral blood volume (CBV).  
  - MR spectroscopy: decreased NAA, increased choline, no lactate elevation.  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) WHO grade 2 bilateral frontal oligodendroglioma  
   Pros:  
   - Typically bilateral frontal lesions; calcifications often present (gyriform coarse calcifications on right lesion).  
   - T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, less often strong enhancement or restricted diffusion.  
   - Increased choline, decreased NAA on spectroscopy is classical.  
   Cons:  
   - Bilateral presentation is typical, but often lesions are more defined.  
   - Bone remodeling is unusual but can occur.  
2) Diffuse astrocytoma (WHO grade 2)  
   Pros:  
   - T2 hyperintense lesions without significant enhancement or diffusion restriction.  
   - Increased choline, decreased NAA on spectroscopy.  
   Cons:  
   - Calcifications less common in diffuse astrocytoma.  
   - Bilateral frontal involvement without corpus callosum crossing is less typical; diffuse astrocytomas are diffuse and ill-defined.  
3) Glioblastoma (WHO grade 4)  
   Pros:  
   - Increased CBV and hemorrhage compatible.  
   - Enhancement can be minimal in some glioblastomas.  
   Cons:  
   - Usually asymmetric, often crosses corpus callosum (but absence here helps rule out).  
   - Typically shows central necrosis and stronger enhancement.  
   - Patient not typical for glioblastoma (younger age, bilateral small lesions).  
4) Ganglioglioma  
   Pros:  
   - Typically in young patients, often temporal or frontal lobes.  
   - Can have calcifications and cystic components.  
   Cons:  
   - Usually have a cystic/calcified mass with strong enhancement; here only minimal enhancement.  
   - Bilateral involvement uncommon.  
5) Dysembrioplastic neuroepithelial tumour (DNET)  
   Pros:  
   - Often in young patients, cortical/subcortical, well-defined lesions with a bubbly appearance, often with calcifications.  
   - Usually hypointense on SWI if calcifications present.  
   Cons:  
   - Typically unilocular or multilocular cystic lesions; here ill-defined margins and cortical involvement less typical.  
   - Bilateral lesions uncommon.  
6) Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA)  
   Pros:  
   - Typically superficial cortex, may show calcification, hemorrhage, and minimal enhancement.  
   - Usually in younger patients, but can occur at 53 years.  
   Cons:  
   - Usually solitary lesion, unilateral; bilateral rare.  
   - Bone remodeling less typical.  
C) Ranking:  
1) WHO grade 2 bilateral frontal oligodendroglioma  
   - Best matches bilateral frontal cortex/subcortical lesions with coarse gyriform calcifications, minimal enhancement, and spectroscopy findings.  
2) Diffuse astrocytoma  
   - Similar appearance but calcifications less typical, and bilateral symmetry less characteristic.  
3) Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma  
   - Possible but bilateral nature and bone remodeling are less typical.  
4) Glioblastoma  
   - Features not classic (no strong enhancement, no necrosis, no corpus callosum crossing).  
5) Ganglioglioma  
   - Less likely given bilateral involvement and imaging features.  
6) Dysembrioplastic neuroepithelial tumour (DNET)  
   - Bilateral lesions uncommon and lesions here have ill-defined margins rather than typical multicystic pattern.  
</analysis><final> WHO grade 2 bilateral frontal oligodendroglioma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old woman presented with epigastric pain and uneasiness since six months. She had no difficulty in swallowing or regurgitation of food. She had no respiratory complaints. An ultrasonography of the abdomen was unremarkable. The patient was subjected to an erect chest radiograph for routine evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Erect frontal chest radiograph showed a well-defined, lobulated, homogeneous, soft tissue opacity (green arrow) in the left lower hemithorax, obscuring the medial aspect of the left dome of the diaphragm. It was seen as continuous with an ill-defined, left paravertebral soft tissue opacity (pink arrow). There was no silhouetting of the left heart border (Figure 1).
 
 A barium (95% w/v) oesophagogram showed unobstructed passage of barium through the thoracic soft tissue opacity. The retrocardiac oesophagus was tortuous and deviated to the left with a normal gastro-oesophageal junction (Figure 2).
 
 An unenhanced CT of the chest was performed with oral contrast, followed by intravenous contrast-enhanced CT with oral contrast in the same sitting. Axial unenhanced images showed circumferential, homogeneous thickening of the lower oesophageal wall with patency of the lumen showing orally administered water-soluble contrast. There were a few tiny foci of calcification within the wall (pink arrow on Figure 3). Sagittal venous phase images showed homogeneous enhancement of the thickened oesophageal wall without areas of necrosis (blue arrow) or evidence of invasion into surrounding structures (Figure 4). Coronal unenhanced images in the lung window showed surrounding atelectasis without signs of lung parenchymal involvement (Figure 5).

1) Oesophageal leiomyoma:
- Pros: Common benign tumour of oesophagus, often shows well-defined lobulated soft tissue mass, usually homogeneous, can cause oesophageal wall thickening.
- Cons: Typically does not show calcifications; usually intramural, without extension into paravertebral soft tissue; rarely cause adjacent atelectasis.

2) Oesophageal lymphoma:
- Pros: Can cause diffuse or focal thickening of oesophageal wall, may present with homogeneous soft tissue mass, rarely calcifications; may show homogeneous enhancement.
- Cons: Lymphoma less common in oesophagus; tends to be more infiltrative, with possible lymphadenopathy; usually does not present as well-defined lobulated mass; calcifications uncommon; no lung parenchymal involvement as suggested.

3) Oesophageal gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST):
- Pros: GIST is the most common malignant tumour of oesophagus; typically presents as soft tissue mass involving oesophageal wall; can show calcifications; tends to cause homogeneous wall thickening with enhancement; can cause surrounding atelectasis due to mass effect; lobulated appearance possible.
- Cons: Rarely calcifications seen but possible; may cause obstruction or irregular lumen, but in this case lumen appears patent; no invasion noted which supports a well-demarcated tumour.

C) Ranking:
1) Oesophageal gastrointestinal stromal tumour: Fits the presence of calcifications within the thickened oesophageal wall, homogeneous enhancement without necrosis or invasion, well-defined mass, and surrounding atelectasis due to mass effect. Calcifications are rare but possible in GIST.
2) Oesophageal leiomyoma: While typical appearance can be right, calcifications are rare and extension into paravertebral soft tissue and atelectasis less common.
3) Oesophageal lymphoma: Less likely due to lack of lymphadenopathy or infiltrative pattern, and calcifications are not typical.

Therefore, the best match is GIST due to its typical presentation with well-defined soft tissue mass, homogeneous wall thickening with enhancement, calcifications, and mass effect causing atelectasis. </analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old male patient complains of long-lasting chest pain, which worsens in the prone position and interferes with sleep. He denies any dyspnoea, palpitations, or a history of trauma. His medical history includes anxiety and a surgical procedure to remove multiple bone tumours from his lower limbs six years ago.
 Imaging Findings: A computed tomography (CT) scan was performed on the chest, revealing two lesions on the left scapula: one on the ventral surface in the subscapular fossa and another on the dorsal surface in the infraspinatus fossa (Figures 1 and 2). Both lesions presented calcium density and were found to have continuity with the adjacent bone, assuming a pedunculated conformation with anterior and posterior protrusion. These well-defined lesions did not show destruction of the cortical bone, irregular calcifications, or periosteal reaction of the underlying bone.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient is young adult male (24 years) with long-standing chest pain worse in prone position.  
- History of multiple bone tumours removed surgically from lower limbs six years ago (suggests a genetic or syndromic predisposition to multiple bone lesions).  
- Imaging shows two lesions on the left scapula: one ventrally in subscapular fossa and one dorsally in infraspinatus fossa.  
- Lesions have calcium density, well-defined, pedunculated, continuous with adjacent bone, with no cortical destruction, irregular calcifications, or periosteal reaction.  
- Lesions protrude anteriorly and posteriorly relative to scapular surface, suggesting exostoses/exophytic growths.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Periosteal osteosarcomas:  
   - Pros: Can present as surface lesions; may have aggressive features.  
   - Cons: Typically show poorly defined margins, cortical destruction, periosteal reaction, and are aggressive malignant tumours. Here lesions are well-defined, no cortical destruction or periosteal reaction. Less likely.

2) Parosteal osteosarcomas:  
   - Pros: Surface (juxta-cortical) malignant bone tumours with a tendency for less aggressive presentation than conventional osteosarcoma.  
   - Cons: Usually affect diaphysis/metaphysis of long bones (e.g., femur); scapular involvement is rare. Lesions often have dense mineralization and may show cortical involvement but not as pedunculated exostoses-like lesions. No cortical destruction here. Clinical history of previous multiple bone tumours suggests benign process rather than malignant.

3) Osteochondromas of the ventral and dorsal scapula:  
   - Pros: Classic exostoses/exophytic lesions continuous with the underlying bone cortex and medullary cavity; usually pedunculated or sessile; commonly show calcified matrix; well-defined; no cortical destruction or aggressive periosteal reaction. Can cause pain due to mechanical irritation, especially in prone position. Scapular osteochondromas can cause chest wall pain and interfere with sleep. History of multiple bone tumours could suggest hereditary multiple exostoses (multiple osteochondromas syndrome).  
   - Cons: None significant; fits well with described imaging and history.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old man with no significant medical history develops back pain after a running session. After starting physiotherapy, the complaints became acutely worse and did not disappear during the night. No other B-symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: Computer tomography (CT) of the thoracic spine showed a moth-eaten osteolytic aspect of the 7th thoracic vertebral body with a pathological wedge-shaped compression fracture. There is an associated homogeneously enhancing extensive paravertebral and epidural soft tissue component (approximately 56 x 65 x 47 mm) with encasement of the azygos vein.
 
 MRI demonstrated diffuse T1 hypointense, STIR hyperintense (compared to the disci) bone marrow infiltration of the 7th vertebral body with paravertebral and epidural mass extension. The mass showed homogeneous contrast enhancement with marked diffusion restriction (ADC value of 710 μm²/sec). A compressive myelopathy developed based on the epidural component. Encasement of the azygos vein is seen, as well as intercostal arteries traversing through the mass without invasion. There were no signs of invasion of the disci or other vertebrae.
 
 Additional CT thorax and abdomen, PET-CT, and whole body MRI showed no other lesions or adenopathies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old lady presented with complaints of pain and swelling in the left-hand middle finger following minor trauma. She has been having on-and-off dull aching pain. She sought treatment in a local hospital with buddy strapping. The blood investigation showed mildly elevated CRP (5.8 mg/dl) and ESR (50 mm/hr) but normal serum creatinine, calcium, phosphorus and alkaline phosphatase.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray showed a well-defined central expansile lytic lesion with scalloped margins involving the base of the 3rd proximal phalanx of the left hand with few specks of calcifications, and pathological fractures involving medial and lateral walls of the 3rd proximal phalanx (Figure 1a). Computed tomography showed similar findings to X-ray (Figure 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the hand showed a well-defined expansile lytic lesion which was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and showed no diffusion restriction with scalloping and fracture involving medial and lateral walls, causing splaying of sagittal bands on both sides (Figures 1c, 1d, 1e, 1f, and 1g).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old female patient, with no relevant previous history, presented to the outpatient clinic complaining of a progressively growing swelling at the left popliteal fossa, with associated limited knee flexion and local pain. At clinical examination, a firm and non-mobile swelling was evident at the popliteal region on the left.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed a subfascial soft tissue mass mainly composed of tissue isointense to the muscle on T1-weighted (T1) sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted fat-suppressed (T2FS) sequences and which, after contrast with fat suppression (T1 Gd), showed intense and fast enhancement, as well as “feathery” margins (flame sign). These areas were mixed with areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, which did not enhance after contrast (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 2). Some diffusion restriction was seen in the more enhancing areas (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 The extra-articular mass extended into the subcutis, as well as into the muscle bellies of the biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius. The mass also encased the peroneal nerve (Figure 4), following its trajectory until the level of the fibular head (Figures 5a and 5b). Reactive oedema along the deep fascia of the lower leg was also present.

   - Mixed areas of very low signal on T1 and T2FS, non-enhancing.
   - Some diffusion restriction in enhancing areas.
   - Extension into subcutis and muscle bellies of biceps femoris and medial gastrocnemius.
   - Encasement of peroneal nerve extending to fibular head.
   - Reactive edema along deep fascia of lower leg.

1) Desmoid fibromatosis
   - Pros: Subfascial mass with intense enhancement and feathery margins; involvement of adjacent fascial planes (reactive edema); slow-growing, firm, non-mobile mass.
   - Cons: Typically does not encase nerves aggressively; usually does not have areas of very low signal non-enhancing on T2; usually does not involve subcutis and muscle bellies to this extent; size/location/extension is more aggressive than typical desmoid behavior.

   - Pros: Soft tissue sarcoma; aggressive infiltration; extension into muscle and subcutis; encasement of peroneal nerve; diffusion restriction; heterogeneous enhancement with non-enhancing areas.
   - Cons: Fibrosarcoma usually T2 hyperintense but may not have the "feathery" flame sign which is more characteristic of hemangioma; age is compatible; clinical presentation and imaging findings (necrotic/cystic areas) could fit.
3) Soft tissue sarcoma (general)
   - Pros: Broad category that includes aggressive, infiltrative masses with heterogeneous signal, non-enhancing necrotic areas, diffusion restriction, nerve encasement, involvement of multiple compartments.
   - Cons: This is a generic diagnosis and does not specify tumor type; would include fibrosarcoma, but also leiomyosarcoma, undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma, etc.
4) Solitary fibrous tumour
   - Pros: Can show intense, fast enhancement; sometimes shows areas of low signal due to collagen; may be subfascial. Typically T1 iso to muscle, T2 hyperintense or variable.
   - Cons: Usually does not encase nerves or infiltrate muscle to this extent; usually well-circumscribed (not infiltrative); flame sign feature not typical; reactive edema less likely; perineural involvement uncommon.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1) Soft tissue sarcoma (most likely due to aggressive features, infiltration, nerve encasement, diffusion restriction, heterogeneous enhancement with necrotic-like low signal areas).
2) Fibrosarcoma (consistent but more specific than generic sarcoma; still fits but we lack tumor confirmation).
3) Desmoid fibromatosis (less aggressive invasion, nerve encasement is uncommon).
4) Solitary fibrous tumour (least likely given infiltration and nerve encasement).

Given no histological confirmation but imaging strongly suggests aggressive malignant soft tissue tumor with nerve involvement and infiltration, the best match is "Soft tissue sarcoma" from the list, which is the umbrella term covering such aggressive tumors.

Examination revealed epigastric tenderness without peritoneal signs. Laboratory results showed mild elevated CRP (16 mg/L) and WCC (11.9 x 10⁹/L), with normal liver functions and amylase levels.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed ill-defined fat stranding in the lesser omentum, located near the lesser curvature of the stomach. This fat stranding was not associated with a well-defined mass. The stomach and pancreas appeared normal on imaging, except for a small hiatus hernia identified incidentally. There was no evidence of free intraperitoneal gas, bowel wall thickening, or signs of bowel obstruction. No other abnormalities were observed in the abdominal and pelvic structures, and no additional masses or lesions were noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old male presented with pain in the right hypochondrium and loss of appetite for 1 month. There was no history of fever. The patient has a history of a successfully treated abdominal tuberculosis, for which he had taken anti-tubercular treatment for 1 year.
 Imaging Findings: An ultrasound was performed, which showed a well-defined round to oval isoechoic lesion in segment IV of the liver, without any significant internal vascularity.
 
 A contrast CT scan was further requested, which demonstrated a well-defined round to oval lesion, measuring ~6 x 4.2 x 5.1 cm, well demarcated from the rest of the liver with the same attenuation as that of the normal liver tissue. It was located inferior to segment IV (Figures 1a and 1b) with arterial supply from a branch of the left hepatic artery (Figure 2) and venous drainage into a branch of the middle hepatic vein. It was seen to cause mild dilatation of central and peripheral intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe due to compression (Figure 3). The liver was otherwise normal in size with maintained segmental morphology and normal hepatic veins. The left branch of the portal vein was attenuated in calibre; however, it showed normal contrast opacification (Figure 4). Physiological areas of altered attenuation were also noted in the right lobe of the liver.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Patient, 2 days old, born at 39 weeks of gestation from a controlled and uneventful pregnancy. At birth, notable observations included the violaceous discolouration of the scrotal skin, bilateral scrotal swelling, and difficulty in locating the testes in the scrotal sacs and inguinal canals (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: In the postnatal testicular ultrasound (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d), both scrotal sacs were occupied by heterogeneously echogenic lesions with poorly defined borders and multiple internal calcifications. These were not considered masses due to the unclear borders. Moderate increased vascularisation was noted on Doppler study.
 
 The abdominal ultrasound showed no calcifications, cysts, or free fluid. The testes and epididymides were difficult to visualise but appeared morphologically normal in the upper poles of both scrotal sacs.
 
 To complete the study, a simple X-ray of the abdomen and scrotum was performed, revealing multiple coarse, diffuse, bilateral intrascrotal calcifications, with no intra-abdominal abnormalities observed (Figure 2).
 
 MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) identified solid lesions occupying both scrotal sacs, hyperintense on T2 sequences with hypointense microcalcifications on T2. Both testes were of normal size and morphology and located within the scrotal sacs. No inguinal hernias were identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Incidental finding in the right iliac bone of a 68-year-old male patient with lung cancer who was undergoing a routine follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had lung cancer, and during a follow-up oncology CT scan, we incidentally discovered a painless mass in his right pelvic wing. CT of the pelvis shows a well-defined lesion in the right pelvis protruding into the iliac muscle with dense calcifications and a rim of fatty tissue that confirms its lipomatous nature (Figures 1a and 1b). On bone window, the calcified nature of the lesion can be clearly seen as an excrescence from the right iliac wing, causing a mild periosteal reaction on the adjacent bone cortex (Figure 2). The scout view of the CT is also helpful in observing the lesion, its close relation to the right iliac wing, with clumped calcifications and fatty tissue in the periphery (Figure 3).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Incidental painless mass in right iliac bone/iliac wing in a 68-year-old male with known lung cancer (context suggests a benign lesion rather than metastasis because it is painless and incidental).  
- CT shows a well-defined lesion protruding into iliac muscle with dense calcifications and a rim of fatty tissue.  
- Presence of periosteal reaction and an excrescence from the iliac wing with clumped calcifications.  
- Fatty rim/pseudocapsule suggests a lesion with both bony and soft tissue components.  
- Location: Right iliac wing.  
- Imaging morphology: Dense calcifications, fatty rim, well-defined lesion, mild periosteal reaction.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Parosteal lipoma  
- Pros: Parosteal lipomas classically present as well-defined lesions with fatty tissue surrounding dense calcifications or osseous components; they arise from periosteum, commonly in long bones but can occur in pelvis; fatty rim is typical; mild periosteal reaction possible.  
- Cons: Parosteal lipomas usually have smooth cortical scalloping without aggressive periosteal reaction; location in iliac wing less common but possible.

2) Osteochondroma  
- Pros: Classic bony protrusion with cortical and medullary continuity; often in long bones; can have calcification/cartilage cap.  
- Cons: Typically does not have fatty rim; lesion is purely bony/cartilaginous; no fatty tissue rim; location in pelvis possible but rare; periosteal reaction not typical; no mention of medullary continuity.

3) Liposarcoma  
- Pros: Soft tissue mass with fatty component and calcification possible in dedifferentiated forms.  
- Cons: Liposarcomas are malignant, often not well-defined, usually symptomatic, no periosteal reaction; no description of invasive features; patient's lesion is painless and well-defined; fatty rim is more typical of benign lesions; location on bone surface less typical.

4) Nora lesion / Bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation (BPOP)  
- Pros: Lesion on bone surface with irregular calcified/cartilaginous outgrowth; can cause periosteal reaction; may have soft tissue component.  
- Cons: Typically no fatty rim; lesion is juxtacortical but usually without a fatty capsule; BPOP lesions are often more destructive/aggressive, no fat cap; histology is bizarre cartilage and bone proliferation without fatty tissue.

C) Ranking:  
1) Parosteal lipoma: Best fits due to fatty rim, dense calcifications, well-defined lesion, mild periosteal reaction, location compatible.  
2) Nora lesion: Could explain bony protrusion and periosteal reaction, but lack of fatty rim makes it less likely.  
3) Osteochondroma: Unlikely without fatty component or medullary continuity.  
4) Liposarcoma: Least likely given benign imaging features.

<final> Parosteal lipoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Usual interstitial pneumonia due to idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF):  
- Pros: Classic HRCT findings of UIP pattern include basal and subpleural predominant fibrosis, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, apicobasal gradient, and sharp demarcation between fibrotic and normal lung. Reduced DLCO and restrictive PFTs support chronic fibrotic process.  
- Cons: None significant; findings are typical for UIP/IPF.

- Pros: UIP pattern can be seen in CTD-related ILD, and the former smoker’s age is compatible.

- Pros: Causes interstitial lung disease.  
- Cons: LIP typically presents with diffuse ground-glass opacities, cysts, and is often associated with connective tissue disease, especially Sjogren's. No ground glass or cystic changes reported, and honeycombing is not a prominent feature.

Clinical History: A 35-year-old man, non-smoker, presented to the emergency department of our institution with a three-day history of left pleuritic chest pain, exacerbated by deep breathing and accompanied by diaphoresis and nausea. The laboratory findings and electrocardiogram (ECG) were normal, and his past medical history was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: On chest radiographs (Figures 1a and 1b), a small ill-defined opacity was perceived in left anterior juxtacardiac topography, associated with a small left-sided pleural effusion.
 
 A thoracic computed tomography (CT) was acquired for better characterisation (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and showed an encapsulated ovoid fatty lesion in the left anterior mediastinum with surrounding soft tissue stranding. The left-sided pleural effusion was confirmed, and a small-volume pericardial effusion was also perceived.

Clinical History: A 35-year-old man, non-smoker, presented to the emergency department of our institution with a three-day history of left pleuritic chest pain, exacerbated by deep breathing and accompanied by diaphoresis and nausea. The laboratory findings and electrocardiogram (ECG) were normal, and his past medical history was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: On chest radiographs, a small ill-defined opacity was perceived in left anterior juxtacardiac topography, associated with a small left-sided pleural effusion. A thoracic computed tomography (CT) was acquired for better characterisation and showed an encapsulated ovoid fatty lesion in the left anterior mediastinum with surrounding soft tissue stranding. The left-sided pleural effusion was confirmed, and a small-volume pericardial effusion was also perceived.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Pericardial lipoma, Pericardial liposarcoma, Thymolipoma, Epipericardial fat necrosis, Diaphragmatic hernia
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 35-year-old man with acute left pleuritic chest pain worsening with deep breathing (pleuritic pain).  
- Normal ECG and labs, unremarkable past history.  
- Chest X-ray: small ill-defined opacity in left anterior juxtacardiac area with small left-sided pleural effusion.  
- Chest CT: encapsulated ovoid fatty lesion in left anterior mediastinum with surrounding soft tissue stranding, confirming left pleural effusion and small pericardial effusion.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Pericardial lipoma  
   - Pros: Fatty lesion in pericardial region, well-encapsulated.  
   - Cons: Lipomas typically lack surrounding soft tissue stranding and inflammation; no pleural/pericardial effusion expected. The acute pleuritic pain and soft tissue stranding are unusual for lipoma.  

2) Pericardial liposarcoma  
   - Pros: Fatty lesion with soft tissue stranding fits a malignant fatty tumor; can cause pleural/pericardial effusion and pleuritic pain.  
   - Cons: Less common; typically more aggressive and larger; pericardial involvement typical but location and encapsulation may be less defined.  

3) Thymolipoma  
   - Pros: Large fatty-antral lesion involving anterior mediastinum, often right-sided; may cause pleural effusion.  
   - Cons: Usually large and more lobulated, not encapsulated; typically right-sided; lesion here is small and left-sided; no mention of thymic involvement.  

4) Epipericardial fat necrosis (EFN)  
   - Pros: Acute pleuritic chest pain; fatty lesion adjacent to pericardium/anterior mediastinum; soft tissue stranding due to inflammation; small pleural and pericardial effusions common; self-limited.  
   - Cons: Lesion size usually small; often peripheral fatty lesion with fat attenuation on CT; matches well with findings.  

5) Diaphragmatic hernia  
   - Pros: Ill-defined opacity near diaphragmatic level.  
   - Cons: No mention of herniated bowel or air-fluid levels; described as fatty lesion; pleural effusion and soft tissue stranding less typical; not consistent with radiologic findings.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Epipericardial fat necrosis (fits clinical presentation, imaging with fatty lesion, soft tissue stranding, pleural and pericardial effusion)  
2) Pericardial liposarcoma (less likely due to size and acute presentation)  
3) Pericardial lipoma (lacks inflammatory features)  
4) Thymolipoma (unlikely given location and size)  
5) Diaphragmatic hernia (no supporting findings)  

<final>Epipericardial fat necrosis</final><|return|>
options: Pericardial lipoma | Pericardial liposarcoma | Thymolipoma | Epipericardial fat necrosis | Diaphragmatic hernia

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A young male presents with ataxic gait, numbness in lower limbs, and difficulty in standing and walking. To evaluate further, MR brain and MR spine screening was done, which suggests compensated hydrocephalus. Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunting was done to treat this. After VP shunting, he presents with headache, with symmetrical weakness in the upper limbs and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: Pre-shunt
 
 There is prominence of the midline cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) space in between the lower parts of the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres inferior to the vermis, displacing the vermis mildly upward and the communication to the 4th ventricle—this may represent a Blake’s pouch cyst, an incidental finding as seen in Figure 1. There is moderate dilatation of both supra and infratentorial ventricular systems without obvious periventricular ooze, i.e., the possibility of compensated hydrocephalus as seen in Figure 3. There is evidence of engorgement of the epidural venous plexus causing mass effect over the cord, as seen in Figures 4a and 4b.
 
 Post-shunting
 
 There is evidence of enlargement of the ventral epidural space showing enhancement in the cervical spine and proximal dorsal spine up to the D2 level, with extension cranially till the foramen magnum with prominent flow voids, likely engorged epidural veins within it, causing cord compression as seen in Figure 5. There is evidence of an enlarged pituitary gland (mid sagittal height of 12.7 mm) as seen in Figure 6. There is evidence of a round dural venous sinus as seen in Figure 7. Given these findings, the possibility of excessive CSF diversion needs consideration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old premenopausal woman presented with dysmenorrhea. Ultrasound (USG) revealed a bulky uterus with multiple fibroids. She subsequently underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy combined with a left salpingo-oophorectomy and right salpingectomy. Six months post-surgery, she presented with abdominal fullness and difficulty breathing.
 Imaging Findings: A chest CT scan revealed numerous parenchymal and subpleural nodules of varying sizes, randomly distributed in both lungs (Figures 1a and 1b). No intralesional calcifications noted.
 
 A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen identified a large, heterogeneously enhancing mass with both solid and cystic components in the left para-aortic region, the aortocaval and left external iliac regions, exerting a mass effect on nearby visceral structures without signs of direct invasion (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 A USG-guided Tru-cut biopsy was performed on the left para-aortic solid-cystic mass and sent for histopathological examination (Figure 3).
 
 On follow-up FDG-PET/CT, the retroperitoneal masses showed no significant fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (Figures 4a and 4b).
PostDescription:

Six months post-surgery, she presented with abdominal fullness and difficulty breathing.
 Imaging Findings: A chest CT scan revealed numerous parenchymal and subpleural nodules of varying sizes, randomly distributed in both lungs. No intralesional calcifications noted. A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen identified a large, heterogeneously enhancing mass with both solid and cystic components in the left para-aortic region, the aortocaval and left external iliac regions, exerting a mass effect on nearby visceral structures without signs of direct invasion. A USG-guided Tru-cut biopsy was performed on the left para-aortic solid-cystic mass and sent for histopathological examination. On follow-up FDG-PET/CT, the retroperitoneal masses showed no significant fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Lymphoma, Retroperitoneal sarcoma, Metastasis, Germ cell tumour, Lipoma, Lymphangioleiomyomatosis, Benign metastasising leiomyoma, Peripheral nerve sheath tumour, Teratoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 44-year-old premenopausal woman with a history of multiple uterine fibroids and a total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy.  
- Six months after surgery, abdominal fullness and dyspnea.  
- Chest CT: Numerous bilateral parenchymal and subpleural lung nodules of varying sizes, no calcifications, randomly distributed.  
- Abd CT: Large heterogeneously enhancing mass with solid and cystic components in the left para-aortic, aortocaval, and left external iliac lymph node regions, causing mass effect without invasion.  
- US-guided Tru-cut biopsy performed from retroperitoneal mass.  
- Follow-up FDG-PET/CT shows no significant FDG uptake in retroperitoneal masses.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  

1) Lymphoma  
- Pros: Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy; bulky masses in typical lymphoma distribution (para-aortic, aortocaval).  
- Cons: Lung nodules in lymphoma usually present as lymphangitic spread or perilymphatic nodules, not multiple randomly distributed solid nodules. Moreover, lymphoma typically shows FDG avidity on PET, but here PET was negative.  

2) Retroperitoneal sarcoma  
- Pros: Large retroperitoneal mass with solid and cystic components could be a sarcoma.  
- Cons: Lung nodules with metastases are common in sarcoma but usually show significant FDG uptake; also, sarcomas rarely present with multiple small pulmonary nodules randomly distributed. Additionally, no mention of necrosis or aggressive features typical of sarcoma.  

3) Metastasis  
- Pros: Multiple lung nodules + retroperitoneal mass suggests spread.  
- Cons: No known primary malignancy besides fibroids. Biopsy results (not given but implied) probably not consistent with typical carcinoma metastasis. FDG-PET negative also argues against active malignancy.  

4) Germ cell tumour  
- Pros: Retroperitoneal mass, lymph node involvement, patient age.  
- Cons: No typical imaging features or serum markers discussed. Lung nodules unusual pattern. FDG-PET negative is less typical.  

5) Lipoma  
- Pros: Benign retroperitoneal tumor.  
- Cons: Lipomas have characteristic fat attenuation on CT, not heterogeneously enhancing solid-cystic masses. Lung nodules unlikely related.  

6) Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM)  
- Pros: Young female; multiple thin-walled well-defined cysts or nodules in lungs; retroperitoneal mass (angiomyolipoma or LAM-related mass).  
- Cons: Lung nodules are usually cystic or thin-walled cystic lesions, not solid parenchymal nodules. No cystic lung lesions described. Retroperitoneal mass described does not match typical LAM imaging.  

7) Benign metastasising leiomyoma (BML)  
- Pros: History of uterine fibroids; post-hysterectomy; multiple pulmonary nodules (solid) without calcifications; retroperitoneal masses that may represent lymph node enlargement or metastases; PET negative masses (low metabolic activity).  
- Cons: Retroperitoneal mass could be lymph node involvement or metastases from BML; no biopsy results given, but clinical context fits.  

8) Peripheral nerve sheath tumour  
- Pros: Retroperitoneal mass possible.  
- Cons: Not typical to have multiple lung nodules; no mention of neural foramina involvement or nerve origin.  

9) Teratoma  
- Pros: Retroperitoneal mass possible.  
- Cons: Usually shows fat or calcifications (no intralesional calcifications here); lung nodules unlikely without calcifications; no FDG uptake expected but lung nodules pattern uncommon.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Benign metastasising leiomyoma (BML) is most consistent: Patient history of uterine fibroids and hysterectomy; multiple lung nodules solid on CT without calcifications; retroperitoneal mass likely nodal/metastatic from leiomyoma; PET negative masses indicate low metabolic activity typical of benign lesions.  
- Lymphoma less likely due to PET negativity and random lung nodules.  
- Retroperitoneal sarcoma less likely due to lack of aggressive features and PET negativity.  
- Germ cell tumour less likely given lack of typical features or markers.  
- LAM doesn't fit lung findings (nodules vs cysts).  
- Other benign entities less plausible.

</analysis><final> Benign metastasising leiomyoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented with complaints of right knee pain for one month and hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth. No history of trauma. The patient was evaluated with X-ray of both lower limbs, computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the knee.
 Imaging Findings: X-rays of knee, leg and foot (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c), CT (Figures 2a and 2b) and MRI (Figures 3a and 3b) show variable bone sclerosing spectrum with shared features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata and melorheostosis seen in right lower limb bones involving the right femur and tibia, right fibula, multiple tarsal bones, metatarsal and phalanges.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented with complaints of right knee pain for one month and hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth. No history of trauma. The patient was evaluated with X-ray of both lower limbs, computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the knee.
 Imaging Findings: X-rays of knee, leg and foot, CT and MRI show variable bone sclerosing spectrum with shared features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata and melorheostosis seen in right lower limb bones involving the right femur and tibia, right fibula, multiple tarsal bones, metatarsal and phalanges.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia, Paget’s disease of bone, Osteoblastic metastases, Erdheim–Chester disease, Myelofibrosis, Sickle cell disease
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 43-year-old female with right knee pain and unilateral hemi-hypertrophy of the right lower limb since birth.
- Imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI) shows a "variable bone sclerosing spectrum" involving right femur, tibia, fibula, tarsal bones, metatarsals, and phalanges.
- Shared features of osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, and melorheostosis.
- Focal or linear sclerotic lesions (typical of osteopathia striata), dot-like sclerotic spots (osteopoikilosis), and flowing cortical hyperostosis (melorheostosis) are present.
- Unilateral involvement with hemi-hypertrophy, characteristic of melorheostosis.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
- Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia: PRO - Fits the described spectrum (osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, melorheostosis) which are types of sclerosing bone dysplasias. CON - Needs confirmation but the combination strongly suggests this umbrella diagnosis.
- Paget’s disease of bone: PRO - Causes sclerotic and lytic changes, can cause bone pain. CON - Typically older age (usually >50), polyostotic and symmetric, no hemi-hypertrophy or congenital onset.
- Osteoblastic metastases: PRO - Can cause sclerotic lesions on imaging. CON - Usually multiple axial and appendicular sites, no unilateral hemi-hypertrophy, age and clinical context less likely primary malignancy; no primary tumor history.
- Erdheim-Chester disease: PRO - Can cause symmetric sclerotic lesions in long bones. CON - Usually bilateral and symmetric, involves diaphysis predominantly, no hemi-hypertrophy, no osteopoikilosis or melorheostosis pattern.
- Myelofibrosis: PRO - Can cause bone changes due to marrow fibrosis. CON - Not specifically sclerosing lesions or unilateral hemi-hypertrophy.
- Sickle cell disease: PRO - Can cause bone changes, infarcts. CON - Usually lytic rather than sclerotic lesions, no hemi-hypertrophy or sclerosing dysplasia pattern.

C) Ranking:
1) Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia - This includes osteopoikilosis, osteopathia striata, melorheostosis which precisely matches the imaging description and clinical presentation (unilateral hemi-hypertrophy).
2) Paget’s disease - Less likely due to age and unilateral involvement.
3) Erdheim-Chester disease - Less likely due to symmetry and clinical features.
4) Osteoblastic metastases - No cancer history or pattern.
5) Myelofibrosis - No typical imaging pattern.
6) Sickle cell disease - Least likely given sclerosing pattern and no typical clinical history.

Therefore, the most accurate diagnosis is "Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia".
</analysis>
<final>Mixed sclerosing bone dysplasia</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female patient was referred to the radiology department in view of a history of recurrent patellar dislocation and knee joint pain aggravated for the last 20 days. No history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: The trochlear groove appears shallow with a sulcal angle measuring 165 degrees (>150 degrees is considered abnormal) (Figure 1). The lateral trochlear inclination angle measures 9 degrees (<11 degrees is considered abnormal) (Figure 2). A trochlear facet asymmetry is seen with a ratio measuring 34% (<40% is considered abnormal) (Figure 3). The trochlear depth measures 2.5 mm (<3 mm is considered abnormal) (Figure 4).
 
 Proton density/Fat saturation (PD/FS) hyperintensity is seen in the medial patellar retinaculum with partial disruption of the fibres (<50% thickness), suggestive of a low-grade partial thickness tear. A mild lateral subluxation of the patella is seen. Grade IV chondromalacic changes (Outerbridge staging) are seen in the lateral articular surface of the patella at the patellofemoral joint space (Figure 5).

 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal sagittal (Figure 1a) and coronal (Figure 2b) uterine ultrasound shows a diffusely enlarged uterus containing a heterogeneous, avascular, solid and cystic mass with multiple small cystic spaces (“bunch of grapes” appearance). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen. Physical examination revealed a flat and soft abdomen with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had complete imaging data, including CT, MR, and PET-CT. All imaging examinations showed a mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
 
 Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement (Figures 3a and 3b). Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal (Figure 4a). The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion (Figure 4b). Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced (Figure 4c).
 
 PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d).

- Post-contrast CT: No enhancement of lesion; mild arterial phase peri-lesional liver parenchymal enhancement.  
- MRI: Mixed low signal on T1, high signal on T2 and T2 fat suppression, slight hyperintensity on DWI (b=800), no restricted diffusion on ADC map.  
- MR: Dilated intrahepatic bile ducts adjacent to lesion, lesion connected to dilated ducts, mild enhancement at lesion edge and adjacent bile duct.

Pros: The lesion is in the liver with peri-lesional bile duct dilation and involvement; mild enhancement at lesion edge and adjacent bile duct fits cholangiocarcinoma typical peripheral pattern; mild FDG uptake is in line with cholangiocarcinoma.  
Cons: The lesion does not show typical progressive or delayed enhancement pattern of cholangiocarcinoma; lesion does not show significant enhancement; calcification at edge may be less typical.

Pros: Rare tumor that could present as a mass with mixed signal intensities due to heterogenous components; calcification can be seen; slight FDG uptake could fit.  
Cons: Very rare; imaging findings less well characterized; peritumoral bile duct involvement is less typical; no strong nodular enhancement.

3) Undifferentiated carcinoma (sarcomatoid carcinoma)  
Pros: Aggressive tumor may show low density on CT, heterogeneous MRI signal; may have mild FDG uptake; peripheral bile duct dilation if causing local obstruction possible.  
Cons: Usually shows early heterogeneous or peripheral enhancement; lesion here shows no enhancement. Lack of restricted diffusion less typical.

4) Hepatic fibroadenoma  
Pros: Rare hepatic fibroadenoma can show mixed T1/T2 signals and calcification.  
Cons: Typically well-circumscribed and less likely to cause bile duct dilatation or FDG uptake; fibroadenoma of liver is exceedingly rare; usually no FDG uptake.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male with anal swelling and bleeding of years of evolution. Physical examination revealed a mass of approximately 20 cm in perianal region, with several fistulous tracts covering anal and perianal area with purulent secretion.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a voluminous low-density gluteal mass with areas of internal enhancement (Figures 1a and 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1-weighted MR imaging showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas (Figure 2d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with abdominal distension and pain of 10 days’ duration. No vomiting or obstipation was seen.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed. Small bowel loops appeared dilated with multiple air-fluid levels (Figures 1, 2a, and 2b). A transition zone was found on the right side of the urinary bladder. A closed-loop obstruction was found with distal ileal loops as content, causing indentation of the right superolateral wall of the urinary bladder (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). A few dilated loops show a small bowel faeces sign (luminal contents resembling colonic faecal matter with gas bubbles). The hernial sac was filled with a minimal amount of fluid. There was no abnormal enhancement of the small bowel loops within the hernial sac. No thickening of the bowel walls was noted. Large bowel loops were collapsed. The cecum appeared normal.

1) Inguinal hernia:
   - Pros: Can cause small bowel obstruction; common site for hernias causing obstruction.

2) Right paravesical hernia:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old presented with dysphagia and post-prandial vomiting since one month. She had a history of accidental corrosive ingestion 20 years back, for which she was managed surgically (an elective retrosternal oesophageal bypass surgery using a gastric pull-through procedure was performed). She underwent bougie dilatation of the esophagogastric anastomosis for similar presenting symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Frontal chest radiograph showed a fairly well-defined, round soft tissue opacity in the retrocardiac region with a broad base towards the mediastinum (Figure 1). There were no calcifications or cavitation within the opacity. The lateral border of the descending thoracic aorta was obscured by the lesion. On esophagogram, the neo-oesophagus (pulled up stomach) was seen in the right paramedian retrosternal region (Figures 2a and 2b). There was no opacification of the native oesophagus. Unenhanced chest CT showed passage of oral contrast through the neo-oesophagus into the stomach. There was a well-defined fluid-filled elongated cystic lesion (max. diameter of 6.8 cm) in the posterior mediastinum, posterior to the heart and anterior to the descending thoracic aorta. It was communicating with the narrowed proximal and mid native oesophagus (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old female presented with progressively worsening holocranial headache over 3 years, occasionally accompanied by photophobia and phonophobia, sometimes waking her at night, and refractory to mild analgesics. She denied visual symptoms, sinus surgery, or head trauma. Ear, nose, and throat (ENT) examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed complete opacification of the left sphenoid sinus by a hyperattenuating lesion, with moderate expansion and smooth thinning and remodelling of its osseous walls (Figure 1a). Calcifications were present within the lesion (Figures 1b and 3). A focal dehiscence of the sinus roof in close proximity to the anterior cranial fossa floor and sclerosis of the posterior and lateral walls of the sinus were seen (Figure 1b and 1c).
 
 On MRI, the lesion was iso to mildly hypointense on T1-W (Figure 2a) and markedly hypointense on T2-W (Figure 2b). Restricted diffusion was observed in the right sphenoid sinus on DWI/ADC (Figure 4a). Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement (Figure 5). Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.

A) Key findings:

- MRI: Lesion iso to mildly T1 hypointense, markedly T2 hypointense, with restricted diffusion (DWI/ADC), smooth peripheral rim enhancement on post-contrast T1-W images, thin dural enhancement contiguous with sinus roof erosion. No adjacent brain parenchymal edema or infiltration.
- Presence of intralesional calcifications.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Sphenoid sinus mycetoma  
   Pros: Mycetomas (fungal balls) typically show hyperattenuating lesions with intralesional calcifications, bony remodeling, and lack of bone destruction; often T1 is hypointense or iso, T2 very hypointense due to presence of metal and paramagnetic substances; peripheral rim enhancement and restricted diffusion is compatible. Can cause bone remodeling and dehiscence.  
   Cons: Usually cause no extensive bone erosion or intracranial extension; patient age and chronic symptoms fit.

2) Isolated chronic rhinosinusitis  
   Pros: Chronic sinusitis can cause sinus opacification and mucosal thickening.  
   Cons: Usually mucosal thickening is soft tissue density, not hyperattenuating; no calcifications; usually no significant bone expansion or remodeling; no restricted diffusion typically; no bone dehiscence or intracranial involvement.

3) Sphenoid sinus mucocele  
   Pros: Causes sinus expansion, remodeling of walls; may cause bone thinning and erosion; can see mucosal enhancement.  
   Cons: Typically T2 hyperintense or isointense (due to mucous content), not markedly hypointense; no intralesional calcifications; usually no restricted diffusion; chronic mucoceles may have variable signal but calcifications rare; no hyperattenuating lesion on CT.

4) Invasive fungal rhinosinusitis  
   Pros: Can cause bone erosion, dehiscence; restricted diffusion can be present; T2 hypointensity due to fungal elements; rim enhancement.  
   Cons: Usually clinical history of immunocompromise or acute rapid symptoms; in this case, patient is elderly but no mention of immunosuppression or acute severe symptoms; calcifications less common; usually more aggressive infiltration and brain edema.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- T2 marked hypointensity and calcifications on CT point to fungal etiology (mycetoma or invasive fungal sinusitis).
- Lack of aggressive infiltration or brain edema argues against invasive fungal sinusitis.
- Chronic, indolent symptoms with hyperattenuating lesion containing calcifications, bone remodeling without aggressive destruction, and restricted diffusion are classic for sphenoid sinus mycetoma.
- Mucocele less likely due to T2 hypointensity and calcifications.
- Isolated chronic rhinosinusitis unlikely due to imaging characteristics and lack of clinical symptoms.

Conclusion: Sphenoid sinus mycetoma best fits the clinical and imaging findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 32-year-old, 12-week postpartum female presented with an incidental finding of bilaterally enlarged ovaries on ultrasound. The patient was asymptomatic at the time of presentation, with no associated abdominal pain, fever, or systemic symptoms. There was no significant personal or family history of malignancy or other chronic illnesses.
 Imaging Findings: On the initial ultrasound, both ovaries were massively enlarged (right 444 cm³, left 120 cm³) and displayed a heterogeneous echo texture with hypervascularity. No significant free fluid or adnexal torsion was noted.
 
 On contrast-enhanced whole-body MRI, bilateral ovarian masses exhibited scattered bulky lesions with significant diffusion restriction and contrast enhancement, without signs of necrosis or haemorrhage (Figure 1). Additional bulky lesions were identified in the breasts, mesentery, intestinal serosa, peritoneum, and the wall of the terminal ileum.
 
 PET-CT demonstrated intense fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (SUV max. 14 g/ml) in the same regions identified on MRI, confirming metabolic activity consistent with malignancy (Figure 2).
 
 Breast imaging (ultrasound and mammography) revealed numerous sharply circumscribed, hypervascular, hypoechoic masses in both breasts (Figure 3). The biggest lesion measured 140 cm³. Mammography confirmed these findings, identifying well-defined, radiodense masses without calcifications or architectural distortion (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Taking account of all the imaging features together, these lesions were categorised as BIRADS 5.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 9-year-old child presented with a complaint of intermittent lower abdominal pain and nocturnal enuresis for two months. There were no gastrointestinal issues, weight loss, asthenia, or lower urinary tract symptoms. The patient had no significant past medical history. During the physical examination, a palpable mass with a hard consistency and non-mobile nature was detected on the left flank. Laboratory studies were normal, including urine catecholamine levels. Tumour markers, including hCG, AFP, and CEA, were also assessed and found to be within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound revealed a solid mass inferior to the left kidney and anterior to the psoas muscle (Figure 1a). The mass was vascularised (Figure 1b), had smooth edges, was slightly heterogeneous, predominantly isoechogenic, with some echogenic foci inside, and measured 5 x 5 x 11 cm in the axial, anteroposterior, and longitudinal axes (Figure 1c).
 
 Further MRI studies (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a retroperitoneal mass inferior to the left kidney and between the left psoas and quadratus lumborum muscles. While there was no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures, it did result in anterior shifting of the psoas muscle. The lesion exhibited an intermediate and heterogeneous signal on T2-weighted images and a low signal on T1-weighted images (Figure 3), with no restriction on the diffusion study (Figures 4a and 4b). The dynamic study (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d) presented heterogeneous and progressive uptake by the contrast product, being more evident in the later phase.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male, with no prior relevant medical history, presents with atraumatic intermittent sharp pain in the lower segment of the right anterior thoracic wall. The symptoms began two months earlier when the patient started exercising (CrossFit), mostly triggered by training (especially abdominal crunches) whilst being asymptomatic in normal daily activities.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging of the costochondral region of the patient showed a normal arrangement of ribs and rib cartilage while the patient was in a supine position (Figure 1). No abnormalities in bone or cartilage contours, nor any soft tissue lesions were identified.
 
 Dynamic ultrasound of the symptomatic area, performed while the patient replicated the movement that elicits pain (abdominal crunches), revealed hypermobility of the 9th rib cartilage, which moved upwards and became pinned beneath the 8th rib cartilage (Figures 2 and 3). This triggered the pain, with a popping sensation associated. When the patient returned to the supine position, the 9th rib returned to its normal position (Figure 4).

   - Pros: Can present with chest pain and rib cartilage inflammation.

   - Pros: Classic history of chest wall pain triggered by movement or exercise, particularly crunches; dynamic hypermobility of rib cartilage with pain and popping sensation exactly matches description.  
   - Cons: None significant; best fit for dynamic findings and clinical presentation.

   - Cons: No imaging findings supporting pleural pathology; pain is localized to rib cartilage area and movement-induced rib hypermobility, not respiratory cycle.

2) Costochondritis (possible, but no inflammation or soft tissue changes)

4) Rib fracture (no trauma or imaging evidence)  
5) Pleuritic pain (no pleural signs or respiratory triggers)  
6) Sternalis syndrome (no imaging or clinical evidence)  

Thus, the most likely diagnosis, supported by dynamic ultrasound and clinical presentation, is slipping rib syndrome.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Slipping rib syndrome  
</final><|return|>
