OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old woman was admitted with fever, night sweats, weight loss and abdominal pain for several months. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the lower quadrants. Past history included HIV infection under antiretroviral therapy, with viral load undetectable. The laboratory tests showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevation of C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal and pelvic (suprapubic) ultrasound (US) revealed an intramural uterine leiomyoma and an intrauterine device (IUD) well placed in the endometrial cavity.
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) revealed multiple areas of nodular thickening in the root of the mesentery and throughout the peritoneum (Figures 1 and 2). There are also signs of invasion of the abdominal wall. Small volume of ascites was identified in the parietocolic gutters and in the rectouterine pouch (Figures 3, 4, and 5).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old woman was admitted with fever, night sweats, weight loss and abdominal pain for several months. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the lower quadrants. Past history included HIV infection under antiretroviral therapy, with viral load undetectable. The laboratory tests showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevation of C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal and pelvic (suprapubic) ultrasound (US) revealed an intramural uterine leiomyoma and an intrauterine device (IUD) well placed in the endometrial cavity. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) revealed multiple areas of nodular thickening in the root of the mesentery and throughout the peritoneum. There are also signs of invasion of the abdominal wall. Small volume of ascites was identified in the parietocolic gutters and in the rectouterine pouch.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Peritoneal carcinomatosis, Peritoneal tuberculosis, Abdominopelvic actinomycosis, Primary ovarian cancer
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 46-year-old woman with HIV infection (viral load undetectable) on ART.  
- Symptoms: fever, night sweats, weight loss, abdominal pain, lower quadrant tenderness.  
- Lab: leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated CRP (inflammatory/infectious picture).  
- US: intramural uterine leiomyoma and well-placed intrauterine device (IUD).  
- CECT: multiple areas of nodular thickening in the root of the mesentery and throughout the peritoneum; invasion of the abdominal wall; small ascites in parietocolic gutters and rectouterine pouch.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Peritoneal carcinomatosis  
- Pros: nodular peritoneal thickening with mesenteric involvement and ascites; typical of peritoneal spread from abdominal cancers.  
- Cons: patient with fever, night sweats, and inflammatory labs favor infection; history of IUD and leiomyoma (benign) does not strongly suggest malignancy; also young age for widespread carcinomatosis; clinical history and labs favor infectious/inflammatory process over malignancy.  

2) Peritoneal tuberculosis  
- Pros: systemic symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), ascites; peritoneal involvement with nodules and mesenteric thickening; neutrophilia and high CRP could be seen, though usually more lymphocytic.  
- Cons: peritoneal TB nodules more commonly fibrinous/adhesions and less commonly abdominal wall invasion; no mention of caseating nodules or lymphadenopathy; IUD less associated with TB; HIV history would increase TB risk but labs favor bacterial infection (neutrophilia vs lymphocytosis).  

3) Abdominopelvic actinomycosis  
- Pros: chronic symptoms with systemic inflammatory signs; actinomycosis classically involves IUD users; can cause extensive fibrosis and nodular thickening of peritoneum and invasion of abdominal wall; typical imaging features include infiltrative masses, nodular thickening, and abscesses; leukocytosis and high CRP fit infection; IUD presence is a key clue.  
- Cons: no explicit mention of abscess or fistulas, which are common in actinomycosis, but invasion of abdominal wall is highly suggestive.  

4) Primary ovarian cancer  
- Pros: peritoneal thickening and ascites can be seen in peritoneal spread of ovarian carcinoma.  
- Cons: no mention of adnexal mass; age less common; systemic inflammatory signs less typical; IUD and leiomyoma history unrelated; no tumor markers mentioned.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Abdominopelvic actinomycosis: best fit given IUD presence, inflammatory labs, peritoneal nodules with abdominal wall invasion.  
2) Peritoneal tuberculosis: supports systemic symptoms and peritoneal nodules but less typical for abdominal wall invasion and neutrophilic response.  
3) Peritoneal carcinomatosis: possible but less likely given infective signs and lack of malignancy history.  
4) Primary ovarian cancer: least likely due to lack of ovarian mass and clinical/infectious picture.  

</analysis>  
<final>Abdominopelvic actinomycosis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old female presents to the emergency department with disorientation. No neurological focality is observed. The patient has a history of migraine and blurred vision in the past year. No other relevant medical background.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows loss of grey-white matter differentiation in the left parietal lobe and hypointensity in the white matter (Figures 1a and 1b), which indicates a stroke lesion in the left parietal lobe. In CT with contrast (Figures 2a and 2b), stenosis of the right middle cerebral artery (arrow), a filiform course of the left middle cerebral artery and extensive collateral circulation in the region are seen (arrowheads).
 
 Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation (Figures 3a and 3b) confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side (Figures 3c and 3d) shows similar findings.
 
 FLAIR-weighted (Figure 4a) and T1 post-contrast (Figure 4b) images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).
PostDescription:

Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side shows similar findings. FLAIR-weighted and T1 post-contrast images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Central nervous system vasculitides, Systemic lupus erythematosus, Moyamoya disease, Neurofibromatosis type I, Cranial radiotherapy, Atherosclerosis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old man presented with a history of weakness in the right arm since a few weeks. There was a history of a road traffic accident two months back.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus showed a pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root. There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2c). The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side (Figures 2a and 2b). There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.

There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk. The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side. There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles. Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient is a middle-aged man with recent right arm weakness, preceded by a road traffic accident two months ago.  
- MRI shows a pre-fixed brachial plexus (C4 nerve root contribution) with diffuse oedema and thickening in C4â¦C6 nerve roots, especially bulky C5 and C6 and the superior trunk.  
- Hyperintensity and thickening of the nerve roots on T2 sequences suggest inflammation/edema.  
- Oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve along its course, with subacute denervation edema in the deltoid and teres minor muscles, and fatty infiltration in supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles indicating chronic denervation changes.  
- History of recent trauma (road traffic accident) correlates with nerve injury/trauma.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Traumatic brachial plexopathy  
   Pros: History of road traffic accident; imaging findings of nerve root edema, thickening, and muscle denervation changes consistent with traumatic nerve injury; involvement of brachial plexus nerves (C4-C6 and superior trunk); pattern of muscle denervation matches axillary nerve injury typical in traction or stretch injuries.  
   Cons: None obvious, findings are classic for trauma.  

2) Brachial plexitis  
   Pros: T2 hyperintensity and thickening of brachial plexus nerve roots compatible with inflammatory plexopathy; can cause similar muscle denervation.  
   Cons: No mention of clinical pain, no constitutional signs; history strongly suggests trauma; no typical infectious or autoimmune history mentioned; oedema more diffuse and related to trauma than a focal plexitis; nerve root involvement is extensive but traumatic plexus injuries are more common in this setting.  

3) Thoracic outlet syndrome  
   Pros: Can involve brachial plexus and cause nerve edema.  
   Cons: Usually chronic and associated with compression symptoms, not acute/subacute weakness after trauma; muscle denervation changes over weeks are less typical; no bone or vascular compression described; imaging shows diffuse edema rather than focal compression.  

4) Cervical radiculopathy  
   Pros: C4-C6 nerve root edema could be from radiculopathy.  
   Cons: Radiculopathy typically shows nerve root compression, not diffuse plexus involvement and no muscle denervation changes in multiple muscles innervated by plexus trunk; trauma history and brachial plexus findings favor plexus injury; and pre-fixed plexus involvement is more characteristic of plexus pathology than isolated radiculopathy.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Traumatic brachial plexopathy (most consistent with trauma history, MRI findings of plexus and nerve root edema, and muscle denervation in multiple muscles supplied by the superior trunk and axillary nerve).  
2) Brachial plexitis (less likely given trauma history and absence of pain).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented with a gradually enlarging swelling over the anterior aspect of the right ankle, first noticed two years earlier. He reported no pain, trauma, functional limitation, or gait disturbance. On examination, a ~5 cm, well-circumscribed, mobile, and non-tender soft tissue mass was observed, not adherent to deep planes and without skin changes or signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound demonstrated a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass measuring 33 × 28 × 14 mm (L × AP × T) within the subcutaneous tissue, completely enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion exhibited a heterogeneous echotexture, predominantly iso-echoic relative to the surrounding fat (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 Subsequent MRI revealed a fusiform, encapsulated mass located within the subcutaneous plane, anterior to the aponeurosis, and circumferentially enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon (Figure 3). The lesion appeared hyperintense on T1-weighted sequences with multiple internal hypointense septations (Figure 3), showed high-signal intensity on T2-weighted gradient-echo sequences (Figure 4), and demonstrated marked hypointensity on fat-suppressed proton density-weighted images (Figures 5 and 6). Across all sequences, the signal characteristics closely resembled those of subcutaneous adipose tissue, consistent with a lipomatous composition.

Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented with progressive dysarthria, ataxia, gait disturbance, headache, dizziness, and a history of recurrent falls. Physical examination revealed hepatosplenomegaly without convulsions or neck rigidity. Liver enzymes were within normal limits.

 USG of the abdomen revealed altered echotexture of the liver with hepatosplenomegaly, consistent with chronic liver parenchymal disease /(Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 88-year-old patient presented with an incidental lung mass. A computed tomography-guided biopsy and a bronchoscopy were performed, but the results were inconclusive. Given the size and characteristics of the lesion, radiotherapy was considered. The patient was asymptomatic and exhibited a normal clinical examination. The third biopsy yielded diagnostic results.
 Imaging Findings: Conventional chest X-ray shows an enlarged right pulmonary hilum and magnification of the cardiac silhouette due to the superimposed right lower lobe mass (silhouette sign) (Figure 1).
 
 Computed tomography (CT) with a soft tissue window shows a right lower lobe mass with an approximate diameter of 38 x 36 x 66 mm, with ill-defined borders and calcifications within (Figure 2). The pulmonary window shows atelectasis adjacent to the mass, as well as bronchial contact without complete collapse (Figure 4). PET/CT (positron emission tomography) shows FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) fixation in the lesion, suggesting malignancy (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior. Surgeons requested a contrast-enhanced CT scan to clarify the diagnosis.
 Imaging Findings: The contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed dilated small bowel loops with an apparent calibre change in the lower quadrant of the abdomen (Figure 1). As no masses or bowel thickening were identified, the diagnosis was consistent with an incomplete small bowel obstruction, likely due to abdominal adhesions—a diagnosis of exclusion. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior.
 
 Additionally, the gastric wall showed thickened gastric folds (Figure 2). Subsequent gastroscopy confirmed the thickened folds, and biopsies were obtained (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Histopathological examination revealed atrophic gastritis and tumoural cells positive for GATA 3, mammaglobin, and oestrogen receptors.
 
 Both conventional (Figures 4a and 4b) and contrast-enhanced mammography (Figures 5a and 5b) demonstrated bilateral diffuse masses with non-mass enhancement. Palpable areas were biopsied in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast—4 o’clock—(Figure 6a) and the junction of the outer quadrants of the left breast—9 o’clock—(Figure 6b). No axillary lymphadenopathy was detected (Figures 6c and 6d).

No obvious free fluid or adnexal lesion is seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old male with one week of sudden-onset fever, abdominal pain, cervicalgia, myalgia, palpitations and shortness of breath. Recent history of travel to Africa for 1 month, and medications for malaria prophylaxis and pre-exposure prophylaxis for HIV. On examination, he had a fever of 38,2°C, tachycardia and right lower quadrant abdominal tenderness. Initial workup was non-contributory, with normal CBC, BMP, chest x-ray, and abdomen ultrasound. With wide differentials, including endemic, infectious and neoplastic conditions, CT of the head, neck and chest was performed and was unrevealing. Serology and lumbar puncture were also performed and were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, and revealed a 3.7 x 3.1 x 5.1 cm, avidly enhancing paraduodenal mass with central calcification (Figures 1a and 1b), presumed to be an enlarged lymph node. Adjacent organs, including the bowel, were unremarkable. There was no lymphadenopathy elsewhere. An endoscopic ultrasound was done (Figures 2a and 2b) to obtain an aspiration biopsy that revealed B-cell follicles with no evidence of malignancy. After discussion, the patient opted for watchful waiting. A follow-up CT done after 4 months demonstrated no significant change in size or appearance of the mass. This was subsequently excised, and histopathology revealed the final diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old woman had unintended weight loss and anaemia for several months. There was no history of bowel or urinary changes, and an endoscopy was negative. A CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed to evaluate for malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in a portal venous phase (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). An ovoid, heterogeneous soft tissue mass with smooth, well-defined margins was present in the left upper mesorectal space, mildly displacing the rectum to the right. It measured 61 x 58 x 45 mm and did not contain cystic spaces or macroscopic fat. The mass did not herniate through the sciatic notch and did not appear along the expected path of a nerve or vasculature. Upon administration of IV contrast, the mass was hypervascular on the portal venous phase. There was free fluid in the pelvis, representing simple ascites, without evidence of complexity or active bleeding. No MRI was performed since the patient was lost to follow-up after her initial CT scan and biopsy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 17-year history of diabetes mellitus presented to our institution with a 3-month history of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms, including urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, and gross haematuria. Notably absent was acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. After hospitalisation, it was found that there were pneumaturia and faeces-like changes in the urine (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal ultrasonography showed a gas-like strong echo and irregular hyperechoic stone-like mass with posterior shadowing in the bladder (Figure 2a). To exclude gynaecological diseases, a transvaginal ultrasound was performed. The right lateral wall of the bladder and the adjacent bowel were connected through a fistula. The fistula tract was about 3.8 cm long and 0.58 cm wide, characterised as a central hyperechoic and peripheral hypoechoic tract on sonogram (Figure 2b). These ultrasound findings supported the diagnosis of vesico-enteric fistula, but the specific part of the intestine could not be clarified.
 
 A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a small air-fluid level at the top of the bladder (Figure 3a). A tubular tract could be seen protruding from the right lateral wall of the bladder up to the neighbouring thickened bowel and this is filled with contrast-opacified urine (Figure 3b). A diagnosis of fistulous communication between the bladder and bowel was suggested following the findings on the excretory-phase CT scan. CT coronal scan showed faecal stones in the bladder (Figure 3c).
 
 Cystoscopy showed an oedematous right lateral wall of the bladder associated with inflammatory changes. It also revealed a fistulous tract opening with a diameter of 1 cm, with pus moss and yellow floc attached to the bladder mucosal surface. The yellow floc is the stone-like mass seen on the ultrasound. The cystoscope could be inserted via a sinus tract opening in the right lateral bladder wall, where the distal tip exhibited bifurcation. Despite attempts, the endoscope could only advance 2 cm beyond the entry point, with further progression prevented by this structural anomaly.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

- Mild pulmonary artery dilation, cardiac pacemaker present.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  

1) Usual interstitial pneumonia due to idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF):  
Pros: The hallmark imaging findings of IPF (usual interstitial pneumonia pattern) include basal and subpleural predominant honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung, as described here. The pattern is typical of UIP (usual interstitial pneumonia) with upper lung sparing and basal predominance. Also, absence of significant ground glass and no other diseases.  
Cons: None significant; the clinical context of progressive dyspnoea and lung function consistent with restrictive and low DLCO fits IPF.  

2) Usual interstitial pneumonia secondary to connective tissue disorder (CTD):  
Pros: UIP pattern can be seen secondary to CTD; fibrosis limited to anterior segments of upper lobes and no mention of CTD symptoms but could be possible, especially with hypothyroidism.  
Cons: No clinical or serologic evidence of CTD is provided; no other systemic signs or autoantibody profile mentioned; also no ground-glass opacities typical for NSIP or other CTD ILDs; the pattern is classic for IPF and not suggestive of CTD-related ILD based on this info.  

3) Non-specific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP):  
Pros: Common in CTD-related ILD.  
Cons: NSIP typically shows basal and peripheral ground-glass opacities, less honeycombing, and a more uniform fibrosis without abrupt transition or marked honeycombing; here exuberant honeycombing and abrupt transition favor UIP over NSIP. Also, NSIP is less likely in this setting given imaging pattern.  

4) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP):  
Pros: Can be seen with CTD or immunodeficiency.  
Cons: LIP usually shows diffuse ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodules, and cysts rather than honeycombing; no nodules or ground-glass opacities here, and honeycombing is predominant, so LIP is unlikely.  

C) Ranking and final choice:

2) Usual interstitial pneumonia secondary to connective tissue disorder (very similar imaging, but no CTD symptoms/support).  
3) Non-specific interstitial pneumonia (imaging less typical).  
4) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (imaging not compatible).  
Therefore, the findings best match Usual interstitial pneumonia due to idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old woman with a longstanding history of rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was being treated with subcutaneous methotrexate 10 mg. She went to the emergency department due to sudden dyspnoea and left chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray showed a moderate left pneumothorax and a cavitated lesion in the subpleural region of the left upper lobe (LUL) (Figure 1a), which was present on a prior chest X-ray (Figure 1b). After a chest tube was placed (Figure 1c), the pneumothorax did not fully resolve, and symptoms persisted, prompting a CT scan.
 
 CT reported a left pneumothorax. The tube was correctly positioned. A cavitated nodule located in the LUL with communication to the pleural cavity was shown in axial (Figure 2a) and coronal planes (Figure 2b). Other broad-based nodules in the pleural surface were identified (Figure 2c). The rest of the parenchyma presented a slight thickening of the interlobular septa with a diffuse bilateral distribution, probably related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old woman presented to the gastroenterologist with postprandial fullness and nausea persisting for one year, along with a 4 kg weight loss. She had no abdominal pain and reported normal faeces. Blood tests were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), a suspicion of a thickened stomach wall was raised.
 
 CT (Figures 2a and 2b) confirmed diffuse wall thickening and hyperaemic inner lining of the stomach with a small lumen. Lymphadenopathies were identified in the gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments. No peritoneal metastases were observed on CT, but the lack of significant intra-abdominal fat limited definitive exclusion.
 
 During gastroscopy Figure 3), insufflation of the stomach was difficult, but no focal tumoral process was noted.
 
 Initial MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) showed diffusion restriction of the stomach lining. Additionally, multiple subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions were identified, which were confirmed via diagnostic laparoscopy. Follow-up MRI (Figures 5a and 5b) showed disease progression with increased wall thickening of the stomach in the antrum, pylorus, and duodenal bulb, as well as the development of bilateral ovarian lesions.

- Lymphadenopathies in gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments.  
- No obvious peritoneal metastases on CT, but subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions seen on MRI.  
- MRI shows diffusion restriction of stomach lining and ovarian lesions on follow-up.  
- Gastroscopy unable to insufflate stomach well, but no focal tumor seen.  
- Disease progression with wall thickening extending to antrum, pylorus, duodenal bulb, and development of bilateral ovarian lesions.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Metastases:  
   Pros: Disseminated peritoneal, mesenteric, and ovarian lesions, lymphadenopathies, diffuse thickening of stomach wall.  
   Cons: No primary tumor identified on gastroscopy, no clear focal lesion; wall thickening diffuse rather than focal mass.  

2) Lymphoma:  
   Pros: Diffuse gastric wall thickening, lymphadenopathies, diffusion restriction on MRI (lymphoma tends to restrict diffusion), weight loss, no pain.  
   Cons: No classic nodal masses described, bilateral ovarian involvement rare but possible; lymphoma less commonly presents with small gastric lumen or diffuse hyperaemic inner lining.  

3) Inflammation:  
   Pros: Diffuse thickening could be inflammatory (e.g., gastritis), hyperaemia.  
   Cons: No abdominal pain, systemic symptoms mild, diffuse peritoneal and ovarian lesions unusual for simple inflammation.  

4) Ménétrier disease:  
   Pros: Diffuse gastric wall thickening, hyperaemic mucosa, small gastric lumen, chronic symptoms of fullness and nausea, often middle-aged patients.  
   Cons: Typically does not cause lymphadenopathy or distant peritoneal/mesenteric/ovarian lesions; peritoneal and ovarian involvement argue against isolated Ménétrier disease.  

5) Linitis plastica in diffuse stomach carcinoma:  
   Pros: Diffuse wall thickening with small lumen, hyperaemic inner lining, lymphadenopathies, peritoneal and ovarian metastases (Krukenberg tumors), diffusion restriction, no focal lesion on endoscopy (typical for linitis plastica).  
   Cons: None significant; clinical and imaging features highly suggestive.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Linitis plastica in diffuse stomach carcinoma best fits the combination of diffuse gastric wall thickening, small lumen, hyperaemic mucosa, lymphadenopathy, and peritoneal/ovarian metastases with diffusion restriction.  
- Lymphoma is a close contender due to diffuse thickening and diffusion restriction, but peritoneal and ovarian involvement are less typical.  
- Inflammation and Ménétrier disease lack metastatic spread and lymphadenopathy.  
- Metastases alone is not a primary diagnosis but rather a manifestation; linitis plastica explains the primary gastric pathology and metastases.  

Conclusion: Linitis plastica in diffuse stomach carcinoma is the most fitting diagnosis given the imaging and clinical presentation.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male patient presented to the emergency room with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness. He had a medical history of alcoholism, tobacco use, hyperlipidaemia, and traumatic splenectomy, and was hospitalised due to an ischemic stroke. He underwent routine diagnostics, where, from laboratory tests, only hyperlipidaemia was clinically significant.
 Imaging Findings: Routine abdominal ultrasound revealed an asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass measuring 46 x 36 mm in the 8th liver segment (Figure 1).
 
 On abdominal multidetector computerised tomography (MDCT), the mass was ill-defined and isodense to the surrounding liver parenchyma on unenhanced scans (Figure 2a). Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation (Figure 2b), whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma (Figure 2c). Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach (Figure 2d) and one in the left subphrenic region (Figure 2e).
 
 Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm (Figure 3a). The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase (Figure 3b), following washout in later phases (Figure 3c). The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI (Figure 3d) and low ADC values (Figure 3e).

A) Key findings:
- 62-year-old male with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness, history including stroke and chronic diseases (alcoholism, hyperlipidaemia, splenectomy due to trauma).
- Abdominal ultrasound shows asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass in liver segment 8, 46 x 36 mm.
- MDCT: ill-defined, isodense to liver parenchyma on unenhanced scan; post-contrast shows increased attenuation initially, then becomes isodense later.
- MRI: well-defined, slightly T2 hyperintense mass, 43 x 30 mm; poor uniform enhancement arterial phase, washout in later phases.
- Significant restricted diffusion and low ADC values on DWI.
- Presence of two splenunculi (ectopic splenic tissue), one near the lesser curvature of the stomach and one left subphrenic.
- Clinical context: history of traumatic splenectomy.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
- Atypical haemangioma:
  - Pros: May show faint or atypical enhancement patterns, isodense on unenhanced CT, could be found in liver.
  - Cons: Typically peripheral enhancement with centripetal fill-in; washout and restricted diffusion are not typical; presence of splenunculi and past splenectomy less supportive.
  - Pass: No
- Liver adenoma:
  - Pros: Can enhance in arterial phase.
  - Cons: Usually does not show washout on delayed phases; typically in younger women; no history of oral contraceptives; no restricted diffusion; no relation to splenectomy history.
  - Pass: No
- Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC):
  - Pros: Washout on delayed phases is characteristic; arterial phase enhancement; typical in patients with liver disease, hyperlipidaemia could suggest steatosis or background.
  - Cons: No known cirrhosis; no elevated tumor markers noted; presence of splenunculi supports alternative diagnosis; mass is hypoechoic and fairly well-defined.

  - Pros: History of splenectomy; presence of splenunculi; lesion with imaging characteristics matching splenic tissue (well-defined, slightly T2 hyperintense, arterial enhancement with washout); restricted diffusion may be seen; location in liver segment 8 compatible.
  - Cons: None significant.

  - Pros: Splenunculi present; accessory spleen could be extrahepatic, but intrahepatic location less typical but possible.
  - Cons: Usually located near splenic hilum or pancreas tail; intrahepatic location unusual; mass in liver segment 8, less typical site.
  - Pass: Possible but less likely than intrahepatic splenosis.
- Metastasis:
  - Pros: Variable enhancement patterns; washout possible.
  - Cons: No known primary malignancy; restricted diffusion can occur but less likely without clinical context; presence of splenunculi and splenectomy history argues against metastasis.
  - Pass: No

C) Ranking:
1) Intrahepatic splenosis (best fit with history, imaging, and anatomy)
2) Hepatocellular carcinoma (possible but less convincing here)
3) Atypical haemangioma
4) Accessory spleen (less likely location)
5) Liver adenoma
6) Metastasis

<final>Intrahepatic splenosis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male with chronic alcohol use presented with a sudden loss of consciousness. He had no history of seizures, fever, or vomiting. Blood tests were normal. Vitamin B12 and folate levels were not measured.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed:
 
 Symmetrical T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in corpus callosum (Figures 1a, 1b, 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5) with T1 hypointensities (Figures 1c, 2c, 3c, 4c, and 4d).
 
 Diffusion restriction on DWI with low ADC values (Figures 1d, 1e, 2d, 2e, 3d, 3e, 4d, and 4e).
 
 Additional involvement of the bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts involving the posterior limb of the internal capsule and midbrain.
 
 No post-contrast enhancement (Figures 6a and 6b).

- Additional involvement of bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts (posterior limb of internal capsule and midbrain).  
- No post-contrast enhancement.

B) Candidate pass/fail and pros/cons:  
1) Wernicke's encephalopathy:  
- Pros: Chronic alcohol use; possible involvement of corticospinal tracts and brainstem; typical locations include mammillary bodies and periaqueductal area (not mentioned here).  
- Cons: No mention of classic symmetric lesions in thalami, periaqueductal grey, mammillary bodies; main involvement is corpus callosum (not classic); no enhancement seen (though may be absent), diffusion restriction less typical.  

2) Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS):  
- Pros: Corpus callosum involvement; diffusion restriction can be seen; typical in chronic alcoholics with rapid correction of hyponatremia (not mentioned).  
- Cons: Usually involves central pontine myelinolysis (brainstem) primarily; cortical involvement (frontoparieto-temporal) is unusual. Lack of clinical context (no water-electrolyte correction mentioned).  

3) Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy:  
- Pros: Diffuse cortical involvement; can be seen in alcoholics; diffusion restriction possible.  
- Cons: Usually more diffuse cortical involvement without corpus callosum predilection; no direct evidence from imaging.  

4) Acute Marchiafava-Bignami disease (MBD):  
- Pros: Characteristic symmetrical corpus callosum T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, T1 hypointensity; DWI diffusion restriction typical in acute phase; involvement of corticospinal tracts and cortical areas documented; classic in chronic alcoholics; MRI findings match precisely.  
- Cons: None significant; matches clinical and imaging findings well.  

5) Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy:  
- Pros: Diffusion restriction; global hypoxia possible from loss of consciousness.  
- Cons: Typically involves gray matter and watershed zones, not isolated symmetrical corpus callosum involvement; no cortical laminar necrosis pattern described; no mention of hypoxic event prior (sudden LOC but cause unclear).  

C) Ranking:  
1) Acute Marchiafava-Bignami disease: Pathognomonic corpus callosum involvement, diffusion restriction, corticospinal tract and cortical involvement in chronic alcoholic, classic imaging.  
2) Wernicke’s encephalopathy: Alcoholic, brainstem involvement, but imaging findings less typical for corpus callosum; typically no diffusion restriction early.  
3) Osmotic demyelination syndrome: Corpus callosum and brainstem involvement possible but no electrolyte correction history; cortical involvement atypical.  
4) Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy: Nonspecific imaging findings; less likely with corpus callosum predominant involvement.  
5) Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy: No typical pattern or history; less likely.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Acute Marchiafava–Bignami disease  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a history of smoking and occasional alcohol use presented with a foreign body sensation and odynophagia. Clinical examination showed a bulge on the right lateral pharyngeal wall with normal mucosa and palatine tonsil. Neurological exam and lab tests, particularly white blood cell count, SR and RCP, were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The patient underwent an MR scan showing a 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space (RPS) (Figures 1a and 1b), bulging the prevertebral muscles and impinging on the oropharynx, with leftward deviation and reduced airway patency. The mass was spontaneously hyperintense to muscle on T1W images (Figure 2), hyperintense on T2W images, with a thin septation on T2 FS images (Figure 3). There was no restricted diffusion on the ADC map (Figure 4), and no enhancement was noted on subtracted T1W images before and after gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b). There was no oedema in the surrounding tissues, and no abnormalities were seen in the pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, thyroid, or cervical lymph nodes.
 
 A comparison with an MR scan from 20 years ago showed the lesion was already present but smaller (10 x 20 x 35 mm) (Figure 6). At that time, the MRI was performed for unrelated reasons, and the incidental retropharyngeal lesion was interpreted as a presumed vascular malformation. Since the patient was asymptomatic, follow-up observation and monitoring was implemented, but the patient was lost to follow-up after a few years.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old male patient with a history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both successfully treated surgically two years ago. During follow-up, a palpable, hard, and fixed cervical mass was detected. No history of neck trauma or radiation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a 6 cm solid and heterogeneous left mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from the thyroid gland (Figures 1a and 1b). This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e). Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy.
 
 A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed (Figures 2a and 2b). There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level (Figure 2c).
 
 A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue (Figures 3a and 3b).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 75-year-old male with prior urothelial and prostate cancer, surgically treated.  
- Palpable, hard, fixed cervical mass on the left side.  
- Ultrasound shows a 6 cm solid, heterogeneous mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularity, not arising from thyroid.  
- CT shows a solid left supraclavicular mass with heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, thick calcifications, and lateral-cervical ipsilateral muscular thickening.  
- Pathological adenopathy with calcifications at Va cervical level.  
- Histology shows spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, atypical cells with osteoid and chondroid matrix formation.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1. Extraskeletal osteosarcoma  
   Pros: Histology shows osteoid matrix formation by malignant cells; pleomorphic spindle cells; aggressive features; typical for extraskeletal osteosarcoma (EOS). The calcifications and ossification are typical.  
   Cons: None significant; location and imaging are compatible with EOS.  

2. Myositis ossificans  
   Pros: Ossification and calcifications; soft tissue location.  
   Cons: Usually occurs after trauma, well circumscribed, zoning phenomenon on histology (mature bone at periphery); no history of trauma; histology shows malignant spindle cells, not benign zonal pattern; clinical presentation with hard, fixed mass and lymphadenopathy argues against benign lesion.  

3. Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy  
   Pros: History of urothelial carcinoma (which can calcify); lymphadenopathy with calcifications.  
   Cons: Histology shows osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue, which is not typical for metastatic carcinoma; also spindle pleomorphic cells, not carcinoma; metastases don't usually produce osteoid matrix.  

4. Benign conditions with calcified lymph nodes (e.g., tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, amyloidosis)  
   Pros: Calcified lymph nodes are typical in these conditions.  
   Cons: No systemic symptoms or findings supporting granulomatous disease; histology shows malignant cells with osteoid/chondroid matrix; clinical history and imaging don't support benign causes.  

5. Another type of high-grade sarcoma  
   Pros: Could present with pleomorphic spindle cells.  
   Cons: Osteoid matrix formation is characteristic of osteosarcoma; chondroid tissue also suggests osteogenic differentiation; imaging and histology are classic for extraskeletal osteosarcoma rather than a non-osteogenic sarcoma.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1. Extraskeletal osteosarcoma - top candidate because histology shows malignant spindle cells producing osteoid and chondroid matrix, which is pathognomonic for osteosarcoma. Imaging findings with calcified mass and aggressive features fit.  
2. Myositis ossificans - less likely due to lack of trauma and malignant histology.  
3. Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy - less likely as metastatic carcinoma does not produce osteoid/chondroid matrix.  
4. Benign calcified lymphadenopathy - excluded by malignancy histology.  
5. Another high-grade sarcoma - less likely than extraskeletal osteosarcoma due to specific osteoid/chondroid matrix formation.  
</analysis>  
<final>Extraskeletal osteosarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. There was a family history of deafness and hearing loss on the father’s side. He experienced relatively few difficulties in daily life. Audiometry showed near-total deafness on the left side and normal thresholds on the right.
 Imaging Findings: A 3T MR study of the temporal bones was performed. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). Additionally, there was no identifiable left cochlear nerve, and apart from the facial nerve, there was only one other nerve, smaller in calibre compared to the facial nerve, in the internal auditory canal, consistent with a hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve and cochlear nerve aplasia (Figures 3a, 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).
PostDescription:

The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose. The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cochlear hypoplasia type I (with aplasia of the cochlear nerve), Cochlear hypoplasia type II, Cochlear hypoplasia type III, Cochlear hypoplasia type IV, Cochlear aplasia, Labyrinthitis ossificans, Bony inner ear malformation (any other type)
FinalDiagnosis: Cochlear hypoplasia type I (with aplasia of the cochlear nerve)

- Left vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into superior and inferior branches.  
- Left internal auditory canal (IAC) small caliber, narrowing medially to laterally.  
- Left vestibule mildly globose.  
- Left lateral semicircular canal focal signal loss (possible artifact).  
- Right inner ear structures are normal.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Cochlear hypoplasia type I (with aplasia of the cochlear nerve):  
   - Pros: Bud-like, cystic cochlea (underdeveloped cochlear turns); absent modiolus and interscalar septa consistent with cochlear hypoplasia type I. Coexistent cochlear nerve aplasia/hypoplasia (no cochlear nerve visualized, hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve).  
   - Cons: None significant; findings fit well.  

2) Cochlear hypoplasia type II:  
   - Pros: Could be possible if moderate hypoplasia.  
   - Cons: Type II usually has a normal number of cochlear turns but shortened cochlear length; here there is no normal turn, bud-like cystic cochlea, and absence of modiolus, which is not typical for type II.  

3) Cochlear hypoplasia type III:  
   - Pros: Some hypoplasia types have abnormal cochlear turns.  
   - Cons: Type III typically has normal cochlear turns but lacks interscalar septa; here turns are absent, cochlea is bud-like, so does not match type III well.

   - Cons: Typically cochlear aplasia is complete absence of cochlear bud; here there is a bud-like, cystic cochlea present, not complete aplasia. Additionally, the presence of a small cochlea (or cochlear remnant) is inconsistent with absolute cochlear aplasia.  

6) Labyrinthitis ossificans:  
   - Pros: Can cause cochlear signal loss.

   - Pros: Many bony malformations exist.  
   - Cons: The described findings particularly match cochlear development anomaly rather than bony malformation per se. The nerve hypoplasia/aplasia is also a soft tissue abnormality, which is well-defined here.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Cochlear hypoplasia type I (with aplasia of the cochlear nerve)  
2) Cochlear aplasia  
3) Cochlear hypoplasia type II  
4) Cochlear hypoplasia type III  
5) Cochlear hypoplasia type IV  
6) Bony inner ear malformation  
7) Labyrinthitis ossificans  

Given the bud-like cystic cochlea with absent turns and modiolus, and cochlear nerve aplasia/hypoplasia, cochlear hypoplasia type I with cochlear nerve aplasia best fits.  
</analysis>  
<final>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old male with a history of recurrent urolithiasis presented with persistent abdominal pain and unexplained fever. Blood tests revealed elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
 Imaging Findings: On the CT scan, the left kidney is markedly enlarged, and multiple kidney calculi, including a staghorn calculus, are identified within the calyces and renal pelvis (Figures 1a and 2a). Diffuse thinning of the renal cortex and non-homogeneous, poorly differentiated renal parenchyma, replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (mean attenuation ~ +11 HU) (Figure 1b), with rim enhancement (Figure 3), consistent with the “bear paw sign”, associated with minimal air-fluid levels. Fat-density areas (mean attenuation ~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to the fluid collections (xanthomas) are seen (Figure 1c). The renal pelvis and calyces are dilated. The perirenal fat stranding and thickening of both Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae are noted, indicating stage II–III inflammatory extension. The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter (Figure 2b). On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function (Figure 4a and 4b).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 29-year-old male with recurrent urolithiasis, persistent abdominal pain, and fever.
- Elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
- CT: Markedly enlarged left kidney.
- Presence of multiple calculi including a staghorn calculus.
- Diffuse thinning of renal cortex, non-homogeneous and poorly differentiated renal parenchyma.
- Multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (~+11 HU) with rim enhancement and minimal air-fluid levels (bear paw sign).
- Fat-density areas (~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to fluid collections (xanthomas).
- Dilated renal pelvis and calyces.
- Perirenal fat stranding and thickening of Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae (stage II-III inflammatory extension).
- Diffuse wall thickening of the entire ureter with fat stranding and linear calcifications.
- No contrast excretion on delayed urographic phase, indicating impaired kidney function.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Renal carcinoma:
   - PRO: Enlarged kidney, poorly differentiated parenchyma.
   - CON: Presence of staghorn calculus, multiple hypodense fluid collections with rim enhancement (abscess-like), fat-density xanthomas, and inflammatory signs are not typical.
   
2) Abscess:
   - PRO: Rim-enhancing fluid collections with air-fluid levels suggest abscess.
   - CON: Presence of xanthomas, staghorn stones, and diffuse parenchymal destruction not purely typical of abscess; abscess alone unlikely to cause the bear paw sign with fat-density lesions.
   
3) Renal tuberculosis:
   - PRO: Chronic infection, calcifications, and inflammation.
   - CON: Xanthomas and bear paw sign are not characteristic; TB more commonly associated with necrotic or calcified granulomas, not the typical staghorn calculus and xanthomas.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>   
A) Key findings:   
- 29-year-old female with episodic numbness, chest pain, diffuse ST-elevations.  
- Echo: severe systolic heart failure (LVEF 30%), basal hypokinesia, apical sparing (preserved apical contractility), irregularities in LV myocardium lining.  
- CECT: no coronary abnormalities, confirms LV wall irregularities.  
- Cardiac MRI: moderate LV dilation, protruding myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, thinning of inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia, severe LV dysfunction (LVEF 20%), GLS -7.85%, ventricular dyssynchrony, non-ischemic fibrosis in interventricular septum and posterior hinge point; normal right ventricle.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC):  
   Pros: Multiple crypts and irregularities in LV wall lining support LVNC; LV dilation and systolic dysfunction are common; apical involvement is typical.  
   Cons: Protruding myocardial bridges are not classic for LVNC; no mention of trabeculations at apex being prominent; basal hypokinesia with apical sparing is less typical (usually apical hypokinesia is present).  
2) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy:  
   Pros: Irregularities in LV myocardium lining could potentially be interpreted as saw-tooth pattern; episodic chest pain and diffuse ST elevations could fit.  
   Cons: Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy is very rare and poorly defined; lacks distinctive features such as apical sparing or classic wall motion abnormalities; no mention of saw-tooth pattern on imaging.  
3) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM):  
   Pros: Presence of myocardial bridges and fibrosis in septum and posterior hinge point; ventricular dyssynchrony; episodic chest pain.  
   Cons: No mention of hypertrophy (wall thickness not described as thickened), instead thinning and dyskinesia of inferolateral apex; basal hypokinesia with apical sparing is more characteristic of Takotsubo or other cardiomyopathies than HCM.  
4) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM):  
   Pros: LV involvement with fibrosis and dyskinesia; irregularities in LV wall; ventricular dyssynchrony; episodes suggestive of arrhythmia.  
   Cons: Classic ACM involves RV with fatty infiltration or fibrosis; here RV is normal; LV involvement is possible but less common; no mention of fatty replacement or RV abnormalities.

C) Ranking:  
1) Left ventricular noncompaction - best fits with multiple crypts, LV dilation, systolic dysfunction, and irregularities in myocardium lining. Apical sparing and basal hypokinesia can be variable but are compatible.  
2) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy - LV involvement possible but lack of RV findings and fatty infiltration makes it less likely.  
3) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy - no hypertrophy; fibrosis and bridge can be seen but thinning and dyskinesia are not typical.  
4) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy - too rare and no specific imaging hallmark described.

Given the presence of multiple crypts, LV dilation, severe LV dysfunction, myocardial irregularities, and absence of coronary disease, the most fitting diagnosis is left ventricular noncompaction.  
</analysis>  
<final>Left ventricular noncompaction</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:

- Surrounding edema in left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa.
- No discrete collection or solid mass.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Osteomyelitis:
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction, cortical irregularity, local edema, and clinical swelling are compatible. Chronicity (2 months), on-and-off swelling.
   - Cons: No discrete abscess or fluid collection; no diffusion restriction which would suggest acute infection; in early osteomyelitis might see more marrow edema and possible collections. Also, no systemic signs mentioned.

2) Battered baby syndrome:
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction and cortical changes can be trauma-related.
   - Cons: No history of trauma; the pattern of periosteal reaction is solid and localized with surrounding edema but no other signs of trauma or fractures; infant has normal delivery history.

3) Hypervitaminosis A:
   - Pros: Can cause periosteal reactions and cortical thickening in infants.
   - Cons: Usually more diffuse periosteal reaction; history of vitamin A excess not mentioned; CT shows localized lesion; muscle edema less typical.

4) Hyperphosphataemia:
   - Pros: Can cause bone changes.
   - Cons: More systemic and generalized bone changes expected; no clinical or lab history; localized region does not fit well.

5) Ewing sarcoma:
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction and edema; age group fits for infantile Ewing sarcoma.
   - Cons: Usually shows a soft tissue mass (solid component) and diffusion restriction due to tumor and necrosis; no solid component or diffusion restriction seen; less likely without mass.

6) Scurvy:
   - Pros: Can cause cortical irregularity and periosteal reaction.
   - Cons: Usually systemic nutritional deficiency with other signs; periosteal reaction typically more subtle in infants; no mention of systemic signs or risk factors; less likely.

7) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease):
   - Pros: Classic presentation in infants with cortical thickening, periosteal reaction, and soft tissue swelling; commonly involves mandible; typically bilateral but unilateral cases reported; no trauma history; usually self-limiting; imaging findings match (cortical irregularity, sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increased cortical width/density).
   - Cons: Usually presents earlier (often <5 months), but 6 months is still in range; no other systemic symptoms.

8) Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis:
   - Pros: Rare malignant bone disease in infants.
   - Cons: Usually aggressive with soft tissue mass and marrow changes; no solid component or diffusion restriction; less likely.

9) Prolonged PGE1 infusion:
   - Pros: Can cause periosteal new bone formation.
   - Cons: No history of such medication in clinical history.

C) Ranking and tie-breaks:
1) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease): Best fits the age, clinical presentation (soft tissue swelling over mandible), imaging features (cortical thickening, periosteal reaction, no soft tissue mass), and localization (mandible angle and ramus). Absence of diffusion restriction and soft tissue mass argue against malignancy and osteomyelitis.

2) Osteomyelitis: Reasonable but less likely given MRI did not show significant marrow edema or abscess, and no diffusion restriction.

3) Others: Less likely based on clinical and imaging features.

Thus, the top diagnosis is Infantile cortical hyperostosis.
</analysis>

<final>Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 2-year-old male child with abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting, clinically stable.
- Incidental discovery of median cleft and rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement in both child and 34-year-old mother.
- Bilateral median cleft of hands with syndactyly of proximal phalanges of middle and index fingers on right hand.
- Additional middle phalanx in right thumb.
- Bipolar displacement of thumb and index finger toward each other.
- Absence of three metatarsals bilaterally.
- Copper-beaten appearance on skull X-ray (multiple convolutions indicating increased intracranial pressure or brain development abnormality).
- Mother with similar deformity: only three fingers on each hand, hypoplastic index finger, bilateral feet with only two toes and possible syndactyly.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1â2 pros/cons each:
1) Roberts syndrome:
   - Pros: Clinical presentation with severe limb anomalies including median cleft hand, bilateral syndactyly, absent metacarpals/metatarsals, symmetrical limb defects; typical severity including craniofacial abnormalities (copper-beaten appearance), growth retardation.
   - Cons: Usually presents with growth retardation and characteristic facial anomalies (pseudomicrognathia, microcephaly) which are not specifically mentioned but copper-beaten skull may hint at brain abnormality/calcification.

2) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM):
   - Pros: Characteristic median cleft hand and foot deformities, syndactyly, absent or rudimentary central rays. Similar limb defects described.
   - Cons: Usually isolated to limb anomalies; no mention of craniofacial abnormalities or copper-beaten skull; parental similar deformity less commonly reported; systemic involvement less likely.

3) Ectrodactyly-ectodermal dysplasia-cleft lip and palate (EEC):
   - Pros: Limb defects, ectrodactyly.
   - Cons: No mention of ectodermal dysplasia features (skin, teeth, hair abnormalities) or cleft lip/palate; craniofacial findings limited.

4) Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (LADD):
   - Pros: Limb anomalies can be present.
   - Cons: No mention of lacrimal gland hypoplasia, ear or dental abnormalities; limb defects usually less severe; not typically bilateral severe median cleft hand.

5) Amniotic band sequence:
   - Pros: Limb defects can occur.
   - Cons: Typically asymmetric and random; not inherited; mother’s similar deformities argue against this.

6) Syndactyla:
   - Pros: Syndactyly is present.
   - Cons: This is a descriptive term, not a syndrome; does not cover median cleft and severe limb deformities; mother’s involvement unlikely.

C) Ranking:
1) Roberts syndrome: best fits bilateral median cleft, severe limb malformations including absent metatarsals, skull abnormalities (copper-beaten appearance), familial occurrence.
2) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM): common hand/foot clefts and syndactyly but lacks craniofacial involvement and parental transmission less frequent.
3) EEC: less likely without ectodermal or cleft lip/palate findings.
4) Amniotic band: unlikely due to familial pattern.
5) LADD: unlikely due to missing lacrimo-auriculo-dento features.
6) Syndactyla: descriptive, not diagnostic.

<final>Roberts syndrome</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 33-year-old female presents with a 6-year history of abnormal involuntary movements of all limbs, face, and lips, which initially started as increased fidgeting and finger flicking. Cognitive decline began a year later, with memory loss, disorientation, and difficulty dressing and eating, and now is fully dependent for activities of daily living.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced MRI (CEMRI) of the brain was performed with 1.5T with sequences of T1WI inversion recovery 3D, T2WI axial and coronal, 3D FLAIR, SWI, DWI, and MR spectroscopy (MRS). MRI revealed atrophy of the head of the caudate bilaterally (Figures 1 and 2) with decreased FH:CC ratio of 1.18 (frontal horn width to intercaudate distance ratio) (Figure 5) and increased CC:IT ratio of 0.25 (intercaudate distance to inner table width ratio) (Figure 6). Volume loss of the putamen and globus pallidus was noted bilaterally. An enlargement of the bilateral frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving a box-like configuration, is noted. There is evidence of T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Figures 3 and 4) seen in the involved region with mild grade II mineralisation seen in globi pallidi, extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and also in the substantia nigra. Mild prominence of sulci and ventricles with grey matter volume loss is suggestive of mild cerebral volume loss. MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA (N-acetyl aspartate) loss in the basal ganglia (Figures 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 8-year-old female with swelling in the dorsal aspect of the tongue on the left side, present since birth, with slight enlargement in the past 6 months. No history of fever, breathing difficulties, or bleeding tendency. On physical examination, it showed a bluish swelling in the anterior tongue measuring 1.5 x 1 cm, and it was a soft, non-tender mass on palpation (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the dorsal aspect of the tongue showed a well-circumscribed hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No evidence of calcifications (phleboliths) or cystic areas. No surrounding induration was seen. Colour Doppler studies showed mild internal vascularity (Figure 2).
 
 MRI showed a well-defined T2 intensely hyperintense (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), and iso to slightly hyperintense signal in T1-weighted images (Figure 5) in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No blooming artefacts on the gradient echo sequences (GRE) images (Figure 4). No evidence of true diffusion restriction. Homogeneous contrast enhancement on post-contrast T1 images was seen (Figure 6). Delayed post-contrast images are not available due to patient motion artefacts.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-year-old child with a diagnosis of Gorlin syndrome, so far manifested by medulloblastoma resected two years ago, and disease-free in subsequent follow-ups until now, presented with insidious hip pain. Given her medical history, a pelvic CT was performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI, performed 2 years previously, at the time of medulloblastoma diagnosis, revealed a mottled intraparenchymal mass in the posterior fossa with heterogeneous enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Actual pelvic CT showed extensive osteolytic involvement with a soft tissue component affecting all included bony structures (vertebral bodies, pelvic girdle, and femurs), with severe destruction of the pelvis and both femoral heads (Figures 2a and 2b). Given the presence of lytic metastases, a subsequent whole-body CT with intravenous contrast in the portal phase was performed, revealing retroperitoneal adenopathic conglomerates (Figure 3) and a single hepatic metastasis (Figure 4).

1) Medulloblastoma with extraneural metastases (fits the prior history, pattern of metastasis)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman presented with a painless right breast lump, present for three months. She also reported easy fatigability, ptosis, and exertional dyspnoea for one year. She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c) due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised.
 
 Ultrasound of the right breast (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3) revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A.
 
 Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female who recently migrated with her family due to war presented with longstanding, progressive motor impairment of unclear origin. She was referred by an outpatient neurologist after multiple sclerosis had been excluded. No previous medical records were available from her country of origin.
 
 According to her mother, she developed normally in her early infancy, walking independently. Around 17–18 months, shortly after vaccination (possibly MMR), she developed dragging of one leg, recurrent fevers, and loss of motor milestones. A verbal diagnosis of post-viral encephalitis was given at the time. Her condition fluctuated, but cognition remained intact. She developed progressive gait decline during her childhood. Wheelchair use began at age 15, and she is now fully dependent for transfers, though she eats and writes independently.
 
 Clinical and neurological examination revealed esophoria, mild tongue deviation, weak facial muscles, hypotrophic upper extremities with reduced fine motor control, joint hypermobility, increased tone and reflexes with bilateral positive Trömner signs, and severe leg-dominant spastic tetraparesis.
 
 Given the unclear clinical presentation, brain and spinal MRI were performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI revealed symmetrical T2 (Figures 1) and FLAIR (Figures 2) hyperintensities in the supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibres), splenium of the corpus callosum, posterior limb of the internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and pyramidal tracts of the medulla oblongata. DWI (Figures 3) showed restricted diffusion with low ADC (Figures 4). No contrast enhancement was observed (Figures 5). MR spectroscopy (Figures 6) revealed a lactate peak at the level of the abnormal white matter. Spinal MRI (Figures 7 and 8) displayed longitudinal signal abnormalities predominantly involving the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts throughout the entire cord. Findings suggested a metabolic leukodystrophy. Diagnosis was confirmed by genetic testing.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.
 Imaging Findings: On MRI performed with the neck in the neutral position, T2 and T1-weighted sagittal images (Figures 1 and 2, respectively) reveal mild spinal cord atrophy at the C5–C7 level. On T2 axial images (Figure 3), atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible.
 
 In the flexed position (Figure 4), there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.
PostDescription:

A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.

- MRI in neutral position shows mild spinal cord atrophy at C5-C7, with left anterior horn cell atrophy on T2 axial images.  
- MRI in flexed position shows anterior displacement of the posterior dural wall at C3-Th3 levels, crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus with high T1 and T2 signal and flow voids.

1) Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)  
   Pros: Progressive weakness in young adult, anterior horn cell loss typical.  
   Cons: No sensory loss common in ALS, but lack of other brainstem or corticospinal tract signs and characteristic MRI findings (dural displacement and epidural venous plexus) are not typical for ALS. Also, ALS typically shows UMN signs and widespread neurodegeneration which is not described.  

2) Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA)  
   Pros: Anterior horn cell degeneration; weakness.  
   Cons: Genetic disease presenting earlier, typically congenital or infantile; usually bilateral and more generalized; no dural displacement or epidural venous plexus changes. Clinical history not typical.  

3) Anterior horn syndrome  
   Pros: Focal anterior horn cell degeneration causing distal weakness.  
   Cons: This is a descriptive term, less specific; does not account for MRI findings of dural displacement and epidural venous plexus changes which are characteristic of another entity.  

4) Hirayama disease  
   Pros: Young male with insidious onset, progressive unilateral or asymmetrical distal upper limb weakness. MRI findings classic: in flexion, anterior displacement of posterior dural wall, crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus with high T1/ T2 signal, localized cord atrophy at C5-C7, especially anterior horn cells.  
   Cons: None prominent; fits clinical and imaging features well.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Hirayama disease â matches clinical age, symptom distribution (distal hand weakness, unilateral), key MRI findings (flexion dura shift, epidural venous plexus high signal), anterior horn atrophy.  
2) Anterior horn syndrome â less specific, does not explain unique flexion MRI findings.  
3) Young-onset ALS â imaging findings atypical, clinical presentation less typical.  
4) Spinal muscular atrophy â onset age and imaging findings inconsistent.  
<final> Hirayama disease </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old man was referred with a four-month history of persistent right upper quadrant pain radiating to the back. Liver function tests were normal, and he denied fever or pruritus. His medical history included pacemaker implantation, malaria, appendicectomy, arterial hypertension, and orthopaedic surgeries. No alcohol abuse or smoking was reported.
 Imaging Findings: The patient first underwent abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b), which revealed consistent findings: gallbladder hydrops with thin walls, moderate dilatation of the intrahepatic right bile ducts, and an ill-defined 1.5 cm soft-tissue lesion at the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
 
 Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 3a and 3b) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts.
 
 Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) (Figure 4) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 A 28-year-old patient with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presented with chronic fatigue, diffuse abdominal pain, and hip joint discomfort exacerbated by walking.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 34-year-old patient with a known history of sickle cell disease underwent CT for left upper quadrant abdominal pain and chronic fatigue.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed a markedly reduced spleen size, with the parenchyma completely replaced by dense, coarse calcifications. The spleen had irregular but preserved contours. Post-contrast CT images did not show any enhancement within the splenic parenchyma. There was no perisplenic collection or lymphadenopathy.
 
 Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Case 2
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1: A 28-year-old patient with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presented with chronic fatigue, diffuse abdominal pain, and hip joint discomfort exacerbated by walking. Case 2: A 34-year-old patient with a known history of sickle cell disease underwent CT for left upper quadrant abdominal pain and chronic fatigue.
 
 Imaging Findings: Case 1: Abdominal CT scan revealed a markedly reduced spleen size, with the parenchyma completely replaced by dense, coarse calcifications. The spleen had irregular but preserved contours. Post-contrast CT images did not show any enhancement within the splenic parenchyma. There was no perisplenic collection or lymphadenopathy. Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities. Case 2: Abdominal CT scan demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Autosplenectomy (rock spleen) secondary to sickle cell disease, Healed granulomatous infections (e.g., tuberculosis or histoplasmosis), Splenic siderotic nodules (portal hypertension), Thorotrast-related splenic calcification
FinalDiagnosis: Autosplenectomy (rock spleen) secondary to sickle cell disease
